  alt: event.name
}]

current_finding.title: robots.txt hardcodes cordivent.com domain
current_finding.description: robots.txt includes hardcoded sitemap reference: 'Sitemap: https://cordivent.com/sitemap.xml'. If deployed to staging/preview domains, crawlers request sitemap from production domain instead of the current host.

current_finding.title: OG images use static SVG instead of recommended formats
current_finding.description: og.svg (1.1KB) is a static SVG. While valid, PNG or JPEG would be more widely supported across social platforms. SVG on some platforms may not render or may render incorrectly.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Convert static og.svg to og.png or og.jpg (ensure 1200x630px). If dynamic OG images are later implemented, use PNG/JPEG output.

current_finding.title: Missing BreadcrumbList schema
current_finding.description: Multi-level pages (/e/[slug]/agenda, /e/[slug]/manage/guests, etc.) lack BreadcrumbList JSON-LD schema. Breadcrumbs help search engines understand site hierarchy and improve navigation in search results.

current_finding.title: Twitter Card uses summary_large_image but no image dimensions specified
current_finding.description: Twitter metadata in layout sets card type 'summary_large_image' but Twitter Card specification recommends explicit dimensions in alt text or image metadata. Current implementation: twitter: { images: [{ url: '/og.svg', alt: '...' }] } lacks width/height.

current_finding.title: No alt text for emoji icons used as images
current_finding.description: Login and register pages use emoji with role="img" and aria-label (e.g., ✍️). While semantically correct for a11y, emoji as content decoration should use role="presentation" or img with alt="" if decorative.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: If emoji is purely decorative, use role="presentation" instead: ✍️ If it's content, current approach (role="img" + aria-label) is correct.

current_finding.title: Static export prevents dynamic sitemap generation
current_finding.description: With output: 'export' in next.config.ts, the Next.js sitemap() function cannot be used (it requires dynamic server-side rendering). Current sitemap.xml is static and must be manually updated as pages are added.
current_finding.fix_suggestion:
If pages are added frequently, consider build-time sitemap generation:

1. Create build script that generates sitemap.xml at build time from route map
2. Or: accept manual sitemap.xml updates when adding pages
3. Or: switch to hybrid rendering (output: 'standalone') with API routes to enable dynamic sitemap generation

For now, document in HANDOFF.md: 'Remember to update public/sitemap.xml when adding routes'

current_finding.title: Could implement speakable schema for voice assistant optimization
current_finding.description: No speakable schema (speakable property in JSON-LD) is implemented. Speakable signals content suitable for voice assistants (Alexa, Google Assistant).

current_finding.title: No IndexNow integration
current_finding.description: No IndexNow API integration. IndexNow (Bing/Yandex) allows instant notification of new/updated pages to search engines, bypassing crawl queue.

current_finding.title: No about/mentions schema for E-E-A-T signals
current_finding.description: No about or mentions properties in Organization schema. These signal expertise, experience, authority, and trustworthiness (E-E-A-T) to AI models and search engines.

current_finding.title: Viewport meta tag not explicitly exported
current_finding.description: The root layout.tsx does not export a viewport configuration via the Metadata API or viewport export. While Next.js 13+ automatically adds a default viewport meta tag, it is best practice to explicitly configure it to ensure compatibility across all Next.js versions and to have control over device-width and scaling settings.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add viewport export to the root layout. In Next.js 14+, export a viewport constant: export const viewport: Viewport = { width: 'device-width', initialScale: 1, viewportFit: 'cover' };

current_finding.title: Missing meta descriptions on some static landing pages
current_finding.description: The Privacy Policy page has a minimal meta description ('Privacy policy for The New Beautiful Me.') that does not meet SEO best practices. Descriptions should be 120-160 characters and clearly communicate page content value to users.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Expand descriptions to 120-160 characters with specific value proposition: 'Privacy policy for The New Beautiful Me. Learn how we protect your data, what information we collect, and your privacy rights. GDPR and CCPA compliant.'

current_finding.title: Static OG image used for all card pages instead of dynamic OG images
current_finding.description: All 78 card meaning pages, 3 guide pages, and 12 blog articles use the same static `/og.svg` image for Open Graph previews. The project has an OG image generation Lambda at `/api/og` that creates dynamic card images but it is not integrated into the card/guide page metadata.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Generate dynamic OG images using the existing Lambda. For card pages: images: [{ url: `/api/og?type=card&name=${encodeURIComponent(card.name)}`, width: 1200, height: 630 }]

current_finding.title: Missing FAQ schema on FAQ-like content sections
current_finding.description: The /learn page has FAQPage schema (6 Q&As), but other pages with Q&A patterns don't use FAQPage schema. This content structure is perfect for FAQ markup which improves visibility in AI search (ChatGPT, Bing Chat, Google SGE) and voice assistant results.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: For blog articles with Q&A structure, add FAQPage schema with mainEntity array containing Question/Answer pairs.

current_finding.title: No image optimization for assets
current_finding.description: The application uses SVG logos and emoji symbols, which is good. However, PNG icon files could benefit from WebP/AVIF optimization, and no srcset/sizes attributes are on responsive images.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Convert PNG icons to WebP format and serve with fallbacks. Consider adding preload directives for critical images.

current_finding.title: No speakable schema for voice search optimization
current_finding.description: The site has rich content about tarot that would benefit from speakable schema markup for voice assistants. No explicit properties are set for audio/voice optimization.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add speakable property to Article schema with xPath targeting key content sections for voice read-aloud.

current_finding.title: No IndexNow integration for faster indexing
current_finding.description: The site has 109+ auto-generated URLs. IndexNow could provide instant notification to search engines when content is added/updated, enabling faster crawling of new pages.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Implement IndexNow submission in the build pipeline: POST to https://www.bing.com/indexnow with host, key, and urlList parameters.

current_finding.title: Minor link text improvements could enhance SEO
current_finding.description: Most link text is good, but some navigation labels could be more descriptive for both SEO and accessibility. For example, abbreviated labels in certain contexts could be expanded.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Consider expanding abbreviated navigation labels where context is minimal. This is a low-priority enhancement.

current_finding.title: Twitter card on guides index page missing
current_finding.description: The guides index page exports metadata with OpenGraph but does not include twitter card configuration. All other major pages have proper Twitter card markup.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add twitter card configuration to guides/page.tsx metadata export with card: 'summary_large_image', title, and description.

current_finding.title: No explicit rel=preload for critical assets
current_finding.description: The application loads assets without explicit preload directives. While the app uses static export and inline Tailwind CSS, preload hints could slightly improve performance.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: If web fonts are added in future, include preload directives. Current system font approach is already optimized.

current_finding.title: Could enhance entity-based content structure for AI extraction
current_finding.description: The tarot card pages define entities well, but could add more explicit entity markup using schema.org Thing and sameAs properties to help AI models understand card relationships and synonyms.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add entity definition to Article schema with sameAs properties linking to external knowledge bases for card references.

current_finding.title: No AggregateOffer schema (future monetization preparation)
current_finding.description: The app currently offers free readings. While not needed now, adding AggregateOffer schema would prepare for future premium features and enable rich snippet display in search results.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: For future monetization, expand to AggregateOffer schema with lowPrice, highPrice, aggregateRating, and availability properties.

current_finding.title: Button element missing keyboard event handler
current_finding.description: The submit button in login.html has an onclick handler but no onKeyDown or onKeyUp handler. While the button element is natively keyboard accessible, the JavaScript function goToGallery() is only bound to the click event. If a user presses Enter on a focused button, the onclick fires automatically, but this should be explicitly tested. However, form submission via Enter on inputs is a keyboard accessibility issue.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Change the button type from 'button' to 'submit' and wrap the input in a form element. This ensures native Enter key submission: <form onsubmit="(e) => { e.preventDefault(); goToGallery(); }"><input id="gallery-slug" type="text" /><button type="submit">Continue</button></form>

current_finding.title: Form submission not keyboard accessible in feedback.html
current_finding.description: The submit button uses onclick handler. While the button element is keyboard accessible, the form cannot be submitted by pressing Enter in the textarea or input fields without a wrapping form element with onsubmit handler.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Wrap form inputs in a <form> element with onsubmit handler: <form id="feedback-form" onsubmit="(e) => { e.preventDefault(); submitFeedback(); }">...fields...</form> and change button type to 'submit'.

current_finding.title: Missing skip-to-main link in demo.html
current_finding.description: The demo.html page has a site navigation with multiple links but no skip-to-main-content link. The skip link should be the first focusable element on the page to allow keyboard users to bypass the navigation and jump directly to the main content.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add a skip link as the first element in the header: Skip to main content and add id="main-content" to the main div or section where form begins.

current_finding.title: Focus order unclear due to programmatic DOM manipulation
current_finding.description: gallery.html uses display: none to hide/show screens (form-screen vs success-screen, galleries-view vs gallery-view). When display toggles from none to block, focus may not be managed properly. Keyboard users might not know where focus landed after screen transitions.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: After toggling visibility, programmatically move focus to the primary heading or first interactive element. Example: document.getElementById('gallery-view').classList.add('visible'); document.getElementById('gallery-title')?.focus(); Add tabindex="-1" to elements that should receive focus.

current_finding.title: Images in gallery.html photo grid may lack alt text
current_finding.description: The photo grid in gallery.html dynamically loads images from an API. The img elements are created without explicit alt attributes: tags are added to .photo-item containers but no alt text is provided. If photos are purely decorative, alt="" is correct. If they are content (which they are in a gallery), they need descriptive alt text.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Inject alt text when creating image elements. The alt should describe the photo content (e.g., the photo title, shot type, or description from metadata). Example: const img = document.createElement('img'); img.src = photoUrl; img.alt = photo.title || 'Gallery photo'; container.appendChild(img);

current_finding.title: Input placeholder used as label substitute
current_finding.description: Multiple forms rely on placeholder attributes as the only label hint. In login.html, feedback.html, and demo.html, inputs like <input placeholder="joanne-garrison" /> or <input placeholder="example@email.com" /> lack explicit <label> elements.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add explicit <label> tags and keep placeholder for hints only. Example: <label for="gallery-slug">Gallery slug</label><input id="gallery-slug" placeholder="joanne-garrison" />. Placeholder should reinforce the label, not replace it.

current_finding.title: Focus outline may lack sufficient visibility
current_finding.description: gallery.html includes *:focus-visible { outline: 2px solid var(--accent); } where --accent is #c4944a. On the dark background (#050505 or #0a0a0a), the contrast between #c4944a and #050505 is approximately 5.8:1, which meets the 3:1 threshold but is on the border of being hard to see for low-vision users.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Enhance the focus indicator with a semi-transparent background or double outline: *:focus-visible { outline: 2px solid var(--accent); outline-offset: 2px; box-shadow: 0 0 0 4px rgba(196, 148, 74, 0.15); } This adds a halo effect for better visibility.

current_finding.title: Form validation error messages not always associated with inputs
current_finding.description: In demo.html, form errors are shown via showFieldError() which adds class 'has-error' and updates .field-error text. However, there's no explicit aria-describedby or aria-invalid attribute to link the error message to the input.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add aria-invalid and aria-describedby to the input when an error occurs: const input = group.querySelector('input'); input.setAttribute('aria-invalid', 'true'); input.setAttribute('aria-describedby', `${field}-error`); errEl.id = `${field}-error`;

current_finding.title: Low contrast on muted text in dark theme
current_finding.description: Throughout the codebase, --text-muted (#5c5347) is used on --bg-deep (#050505). The contrast ratio is approximately 3.2:1, which fails the AA standard of 4.5:1 for normal text. This affects body copy in cards, descriptions, and secondary text.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Increase contrast by lightening --text-muted from #5c5347 to #8a7f74 or darkening the background. Retest contrast: #8a7f74 on #050505 = 6.8:1, which meets AA. Update :root { --text-muted: #8a7f74; }

current_finding.title: Label text not included in accessible name for some buttons
current_finding.description: In gallery.html, style-switcher pill buttons show labels like 'Vintage', 'High Contrast', etc. but don't have aria-label. The text is visible, so the button's accessible name is derived from the text content, which is correct. However, the button in admin mode (admin-btn) may not have clear labels.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: For buttons without visible text or icon-only buttons, add aria-label. For style pills, the visible text is sufficient. Example: <button class="style-pill" aria-label="Apply Vintage style">Vintage</button>

current_finding.title: Gallery card hover state may cause unexpected context change
current_finding.description: In gallery.html, .gallery-card:hover { transform: translateY(-4px); } applies a visual hover effect. This is not a context change (no navigation or focus event triggers). However, the card itself is clickable, and clicking navigates to the gallery. The hover is purely visual.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: No fix needed. The hover effect is visual only and does not trigger any context changes. Consider adding focus state styling for keyboard users: .gallery-card:focus-visible { outline: ... }

current_finding.title: Potential duplicate or missing ID attributes
current_finding.description: In demo.html and gallery.html, elements are dynamically created via JavaScript. If IDs are generated without ensuring uniqueness (e.g., creating multiple elements with id='item-1'), this violates the parsing requirement of WCAG 4.1.1.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Ensure all dynamically generated IDs are unique. Use timestamps or counters: const uniqueId = `element-${Date.now()}-${Math.random()}`; or use data attributes instead of IDs for JS hooks: data-field="name" instead of id="fb-name".

current_finding.title: Focus indicators removed by outline: none without replacement
current_finding.file: dashboard/ada.html, dashboard/admin.html, dashboard/backoffice.html, dashboard/seo.html, dashboard/compliance.html, dashboard/product.html, dashboard/self-audit.html, dashboard/qa.html
current_finding.description: Multiple dashboard HTML files use CSS `outline: none;` on interactive elements without providing an equivalent visual focus indicator. While some have `border-color` changes on focus, this is insufficient for keyboard navigation accessibility. The outline property removal must be replaced with explicit focus-visible styling that provides clear visual feedback.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add explicit focus-visible styling with visible outline or box-shadow. Example: `.filter-bar select:focus, .filter-bar input:focus { outline: 2px solid var(--accent); outline-offset: 2px; }` or use box-shadow for design consistency.

current_finding.title: Potential positive tabindex values could disrupt focus order
current_finding.description: While the current codebase appears to avoid explicit positive tabindex values, interactive elements rely on default focus order. The complex layouts with CSS grid and flexbox could result in visual vs. logical focus order mismatches, especially in responsive layouts. This requires verification across all interactive elements.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Audit focus order by tabbing through each page with keyboard only. Verify tab order matches visual left-to-right, top-to-bottom flow. If needed, explicitly set tabindex="0" on custom interactive elements that don't receive focus naturally.

current_finding.title: Animation pulse effect lacks motion preference support
current_finding.description: The `.header-meta .live-dot` element uses a `pulse` animation that continuously loops with opacity and box-shadow changes. This animation does not respect the `prefers-reduced-motion` media query, which means users who have requested reduced motion for accessibility reasons (often due to vestibular disorders) will still see the animation.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Wrap animation in @media query: `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) { .header-meta .live-dot { animation: none; opacity: 1; } }`. Add this rule after the @keyframes definition.

current_finding.title: Form inputs may not meet minimum touch target size
current_finding.description: While the current dashboard appears to be primarily desktop-focused, form inputs use `padding: 0.45rem 0.75rem` which translates to approximately 28-32 CSS pixels for form element height. On mobile or when considering touch accessibility, this may fall below the recommended 44x44 pixel touch target minimum.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: For desktop, current size is acceptable. For mobile, add responsive padding: `@media (max-width: 640px) { .filter-bar input, .filter-bar select { padding: 0.75rem 1rem; min-height: 44px; } }`

current_finding.title: Language attribute present but limited to single language
current_finding.description: All dashboard files correctly include `<html lang="en">`, which is positive. However, if any embedded content or specific text sections use different languages (e.g., code snippets, technical terms, or internationalized content), they lack language override attributes. This is a minor issue for English-dominant dashboards but worth noting.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: If non-English content is added in future, wrap it with language attributes: `contenido`. This is informational/preventative.

current_finding.title: Tab-based interface lacks complete ARIA tab pattern
current_finding.description: The `.tab-btn` elements in ada.html function as tabs for switching between content panels (.tab-panel). The implementation is functional but lacks complete ARIA tab pattern attributes (role="tablist", role="tab", aria-selected, aria-controls). This may cause screen reader users to not understand the tab pattern structure.
current_finding.fix_suggestion:
Add ARIA attributes: `
...
...
`

current_finding.title: Unlock modal password field has no visible or programmatic label
current_finding.description: The unlock gate renders a required password input with only placeholder text. Placeholders are not a sufficient label and disappear as soon as the user starts typing.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add an associated label, for example: <label htmlFor="unlock-password">Password</label><input id="unlock-password" ... />

current_finding.title: Settings current-password field is unlabeled
current_finding.description: The change-password form shows a required current-password input without a visible label or another accessible naming mechanism.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add a label tied to the field: <label htmlFor="current-password">Current password</label><input id="current-password" ... />

current_finding.title: Settings new-password field is unlabeled
current_finding.description: The second required password field also relies on placeholder text instead of a persistent label.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add a visible label and id pair, such as <label htmlFor="new-password">New password</label><input id="new-password" ... />

current_finding.title: Unlock modal is not exposed as a dialog
current_finding.description: The unlock overlay is visually modal, but the wrapper lacks dialog semantics such as role="dialog", aria-modal="true", and an accessible label reference.
current_finding.fix_suggestion:
Mark the container as a dialog and tie it to the heading, for example:
...

Unlock Journal

current_finding.title: Encryption recovery overlay is not exposed as a dialog
current_finding.description: The password-recovery overlay uses fixed-position modal styling but does not provide dialog role/state information to assistive technology.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add role="dialog", aria-modal="true", and aria-labelledby tied to the modal heading. This modal should also move focus into the dialog on open.

current_finding.title: Recovery codes overlay is not exposed as a dialog
current_finding.description: The recovery-codes confirmation screen presents as a modal but does not identify itself as one to assistive technologies.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add dialog semantics: role="dialog", aria-modal="true", and aria-labelledby on the modal container, plus a heading id.

current_finding.title: Journal editor labels are not associated with their fields
current_finding.description: The journal composer renders visible labels for Reflection, Prayer Requests, and Tags, but the labels have no htmlFor and the controls have no ids. The visible prompts are not programmatically tied to the controls.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Assign ids to the textarea and input controls and connect each visible label with htmlFor, or use aria-labelledby where appropriate.

current_finding.title: Verse reflection editor labels are not associated with their controls
current_finding.description: The reflection form uses presentational labels for Title, Reference, Version, Verse, Prompt, Reflection, Prayer Requests, and Tags, but none of those labels are programmatically associated with the related inputs, textareas, or select.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add stable ids to each form control and convert each visible label into a proper label association with htmlFor.

current_finding.title: Books browser accordion buttons do not expose expanded state
current_finding.description: Each book row is a custom disclosure button, but the toggle does not report aria-expanded or identify the controlled panel.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add aria-expanded={expandedBook === book.bookid}, aria-controls for the chapter panel id, and assign that id to the revealed panel.

current_finding.title: Reading plan disclosure buttons do not expose expanded state
current_finding.description: Plan cards are expanded and collapsed with custom buttons, but those controls do not announce whether the card is open or closed.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Set aria-expanded={isExpanded}, aria-controls on the header button, and add the matching id to the expanded detail region.

current_finding.title: History disclosure buttons do not expose expanded state
current_finding.description: The history screen uses custom toggle buttons for reflection and study cards without aria-expanded or a controlled-region relationship.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Apply aria-expanded and aria-controls to each header button and give each expandable region a unique id.

current_finding.title: Journal entry disclosure buttons do not expose expanded state
current_finding.description: Journal cards have custom expand/collapse controls, but those buttons do not communicate state to assistive technologies.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add aria-expanded and aria-controls to the trigger buttons and an id to the expandable panel.

current_finding.title: Reflection disclosure buttons do not expose expanded state
current_finding.description: Saved reflection cards are expanded with custom buttons, but those controls lack aria-expanded and aria-controls.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Expose disclosure state with aria-expanded and point aria-controls to the corresponding expanded content container.

current_finding.title: Study book search field relies on placeholder text instead of a label
current_finding.description: The book-search input inside the study passage picker has no label or aria-label, so its purpose depends entirely on placeholder text.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add a visible or screen-reader label, for example <label htmlFor="study-book-search" className="sr-only">Search books</label><input id="study-book-search" ... />

current_finding.title: Study chat replies and loading status are not announced
current_finding.description: The follow-up chat appends assistant replies and a loading message to a generic scroll container without aria-live, role="status", or role="log".
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Expose the message list as a live log, for example <div role="log" aria-live="polite" aria-relevant="additions text">, and mark the loading line as role="status".

current_finding.title: Login form omits autocomplete tokens for personal-data fields
current_finding.description: The sign-in form does not identify the email and password fields with appropriate autocomplete tokens.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add autoComplete="email" to the email field and autoComplete="current-password" to the password field.

current_finding.title: Registration form omits autocomplete tokens for name, email, and password fields
current_finding.description: The create-account form does not expose input purpose for the personal-data fields used during registration.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Use autoComplete="name", autoComplete="email", and autoComplete="new-password" on the registration inputs. The confirmation field should also use autoComplete="new-password".

current_finding.title: Forgot-password email field omits autocomplete
current_finding.description: The reset-request form asks for an email address but does not identify it with an autocomplete token.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add autoComplete="email" to the email input.

current_finding.title: Reset-password form omits autocomplete tokens for email, code, and new-password fields
current_finding.description: The password-reset completion form does not expose the purpose of the email, one-time code, or new-password inputs.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add autoComplete="email", autoComplete="one-time-code", and autoComplete="new-password" to the relevant fields. The confirmation field should also use autoComplete="new-password".

current_finding.title: Inline links rely on color alone - insufficient contrast cue
current_finding.description: Paragraph text contains links styled only by color (var(--accent) #c4944a) with no underlines or other visual indicators. Text-only users cannot distinguish links from surrounding text. This violates WCAG 1.4.1 which requires information not be conveyed by color alone.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add underlines to links in main content areas: main a, .prose a { text-decoration: underline; text-underline-offset: 0.25em; }. Keep navigation and button links without underlines.

current_finding.title: Blog filter results not announced to screen readers
current_finding.description: When users filter blog posts by keyword, product, category, or tag, the results update dynamically but there is no aria-live region to announce the changes to screen reader users. Users relying on assistive technology will not know that the visible results have changed.
current_finding.fix_suggestion:
Add aria-live='polite' and role='status' to #blog-results-meta:
Showing {posts.length} posts

current_finding.title: Blog search input label inadequate for understanding field purpose
current_finding.description: The search input has only 'Keyword' as its label, with the actual description buried in placeholder text that disappears on focus. This violates WCAG 2.4.4 which requires link purpose to be clear. The label should describe what kind of search is performed.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Use a more descriptive label:

current_finding.title: Navigation dropdown menu may trap keyboard focus
current_finding.description: The navigation dropdown uses CSS :hover/:focus-within for visibility, but on mobile (< 960px) the menu becomes static with no keyboard management. When opened via keyboard, there's no focus trap protection, and users cannot close the menu with Escape key.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add JavaScript: (1) Move focus to first nav link when menu opens, (2) Handle Escape key to close menu, (3) Add aria-controls='nav-links' to toggle button and id='nav-links' to nav element.

current_finding.title: Navigation toggle button missing aria-controls attribute
current_finding.description: The .nav-toggle button lacks aria-controls to indicate which element it controls. While it has aria-label and aria-expanded, the missing aria-controls breaks the semantic connection between the button and the menu.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add aria-controls: <button class='nav-toggle' aria-label='Toggle menu' aria-expanded='false' aria-controls='nav-links'> and <nav class='nav-links' id='nav-links'>

current_finding.title: Mobile navigation menu opens without moving focus
current_finding.description: When the mobile navigation menu opens (via nav-toggle), focus remains on the button rather than moving to the first menu item. This violates WCAG 2.4.3 best practices for focus order management.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: When .nav-links.open is applied, focus the first anchor tag: navLinks.querySelector('a')?.focus();

current_finding.title: Blog filter form lacks instruction text explaining filter behavior
current_finding.description: The blog filtering form has three filters (Keyword, Product, Topic) but no text explaining how they work together (AND vs OR logic). Users, especially those with cognitive disabilities, may not understand the filtering mechanism.
current_finding.fix_suggestion:
Add:

Filter posts by selecting any combination. All selected filters must match:

current_finding.title: Text spacing adjustments not verified for WCAG 1.4.12 compliance
current_finding.description: The site has not been verified to remain accessible when text spacing is adjusted per WCAG 1.4.12 (line-height 1.5x, letter-spacing 0.12em, word-spacing 0.16em, paragraph spacing 2x).
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Test the site at 200% zoom and with text spacing adjustments. Verify no horizontal scrolling or content loss occurs. Update CSS comments to confirm WCAG 1.4.12 compliance.

current_finding.title: Button border contrast should be verified against secondary backgrounds
current_finding.description: Buttons use a 1px border in var(--accent) #c4944a, which should be tested for sufficient contrast against all background colors used throughout the site, including secondary backgrounds like #0f0f0f.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Verify contrast ratios using WebAIM Contrast Checker. If below 3:1 against secondary backgrounds, increase border width to 2px or adjust color.

current_finding.title: Blog posts lack unique IDs for maintainability and accessibility
current_finding.description: Blog post elements use data-post-card attribute but lack unique id attributes. This can cause maintainability issues and prevent future developers from easily targeting specific posts.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add unique IDs: <a href={`/blog/${post.id}`} id='post-{post.id}' class='post-card fade-in' data-post-card ... >

current_finding.title: External links lack visual indicator of opening in new window
current_finding.description: Links with target='_blank' (e.g., external product links in footer) lack visual indication that they open in a new window, potentially surprising users.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add CSS icon: a[target='_blank']::after { content: ' ↗'; font-size: 0.75em; margin-left: 0.25rem; }

current_finding.title: Blog search input should have autocomplete='off' for search queries
current_finding.description: The blog search input lacks an autocomplete attribute. While not required for non-personal-data fields, it's a best practice to explicitly set autocomplete='off' for search inputs.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add autocomplete='off': <input id='blog-search' type='search' name='q' autocomplete='off' ... />

current_finding.title: Navigation dropdown menu not dismissible with Escape key
current_finding.description: The dropdown menu uses CSS for visibility but has no JavaScript handler to close it when Escape is pressed. This violates best practices for accessible dropdown menus.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add: document.addEventListener('keydown', (e) => { if (e.key === 'Escape') { document.querySelectorAll('.nav-dropdown').forEach(d => d.classList.remove('open')); } });

current_finding.title: Navigation links not keyboard accessible
current_finding.description: The primary navigation links use onclick handlers with href='#' instead of proper links. This creates keyboard accessibility issues as the elements don't respond properly to Enter/Space key activation through standard keyboard navigation patterns.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Convert to proper button elements or use actual href links instead of onclick with hash hrefs

current_finding.title: Modal overlay has focus trap without escape key handler
current_finding.description: The deployment confirmation modal lacks keyboard escape mechanism documented in code. Keyboard users may become trapped when the modal is displayed.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add keyboard event listener to handle Escape key for modal dismissal

current_finding.title: Missing skip navigation link
current_finding.description: The page has a sticky navigation bar that remains visible but lacks a skip-to-main-content link. Users who tab through the interface must pass through all navigation items before reaching the main content.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add a skip link as the first focusable element on the page that links to main content with id='main-content'

current_finding.title: Modal not properly marked with ARIA attributes
current_finding.description: The deployment confirmation modal lacks critical ARIA attributes (role='dialog', aria-modal='true', aria-labelledby) that inform assistive technologies about its purpose and semantic meaning.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add ARIA attributes: role='dialog', aria-modal='true', and aria-labelledby pointing to modal heading ID

current_finding.title: Form error messages rely on color alone
current_finding.description: The form error display uses only color (red background and text) with no text indicator or icon to convey error state. CSS class form-error only applies color styling without any supplementary text indicator.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add a visual error indicator such as an icon or text prefix like 'Error: ' before the error message text

current_finding.title: Focus indicator not visible on form inputs

current_finding.title: No cookie consent banner or cookie consent mechanism
current_finding.description: The application uses Plausible analytics on the marketing site without a cookie/consent banner to allow users to opt-in or opt-out. GDPR requires explicit, freely given, specific, and informed consent before placing non-essential cookies. The privacy policy discloses analytics usage but there is no mechanism for users to manage consent preferences, withdraw consent, or access a cookie preferences center. Without a functional cookie consent system, the site cannot demonstrate lawful basis for non-essential tracking.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Integrate a GDPR-compliant cookie consent library (e.g., cookie-consent, Cookiebot free tier, or OneTrust basic). 2. Add a cookie banner to all pages that appears before analytics script is executed. 3. Make consent granular: Essential (always enabled), Analytics (opt-in), Marketing (opt-in). 4. Provide a way to withdraw/modify consent in footer or settings. 5. Update privacy policy with specific cookie names, purposes, and retention periods. 6. Load analytics script conditionally only after consent is granted.
current_finding.impact: Violation of GDPR Article 7 (conditions for consent) and ePrivacy Directive (requirement for prior consent before placing cookies). EU data protection authorities can issue fines up to €10M or 2% of global turnover, whichever is higher. Users may file complaints with DPAs. Search engines may deprioritize sites without lawful cookie consent.

current_finding.title: Privacy policy missing critical disclosures required by GDPR Art. 13/14
current_finding.description: The privacy policies (marketing/src/pages/privacy.astro and frontend/privacy.html) lack several mandatory disclosures under GDPR Articles 13 and 14: (1) No explicit legal basis for each processing activity (e.g., 'necessary for contract', 'legitimate interest', etc.). (2) No data retention periods specified for specific data types (galleries, comments, access logs). (3) No Data Protection Officer (DPO) contact information if applicable. (4) No explicit breach notification procedures or how data subjects can report a breach. (5) No information about automated decision-making or profiling. (6) No mention of recipients of personal data beyond 'AWS infrastructure' (SES, Plausible, Resend). (7) No explicit right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Rewrite privacy policy to explicitly state legal basis for each processing activity (e.g., 'Client account management is necessary for contract performance', 'Analytics are based on legitimate interest in service improvement'). 2. Add specific retention periods for each data type: access logs (1 year TTL), comments (30 days pending review, then 90 days archived), demo sessions (1 year TTL). 3. List all third parties with explicit links to their privacy policies (Plausible, Resend, AWS). 4. Add section on data subjects' rights (access, rectification, erasure, portability, object, automated decision-making). 5. Include DPO contact (if applicable) or responsible person for privacy inquiries. 6. Document your breach notification procedures and provide contact email for breach reports.
current_finding.impact: Violation of GDPR Articles 13 and 14 regarding transparency. Data subjects have the right to clear, transparent information about how their data is processed. Lack of detailed disclosures can result in DPA enforcement, administrative fines (€10-20M or 2-4% of turnover), and erosion of user trust.

current_finding.title: GDPR data subject access (Art. 15) endpoint requires super-admin key, not accessible to data subjects
current_finding.description: GDPR Article 15 gives individuals the right to obtain confirmation of whether their data is being processed and to receive a copy of that data. The `/admin/gdpr/search` endpoint exists and returns all personal data for an email, but it requires the super-admin X-Admin-Key header. This means individual data subjects cannot self-serve access their data—they must contact the operator (photographer) who then must retrieve the data and manually compile it. The endpoint should be publicly accessible (or behind authentication that any individual can use) so data subjects can exercise their right independently.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Create a public, unauthenticated endpoint (or one behind email-based verification) that allows individuals to request their data: POST /gdpr/access-request with email. 2. Implement email verification (send link to confirm email ownership). 3. Upon verification, return a full data export (JSON or CSV) including all records from all tables, not just samples. 4. Ensure response time meets GDPR requirement (45 days, extendable by 2 months). 5. Log the request and response delivery. Alternatively, require pre-existing client account login to access /account/export-my-data endpoint.
current_finding.impact: Non-compliance with GDPR Article 15 right of access. Data subjects have the legal right to request and receive their personal data without excessive delay. Failing to provide an accessible mechanism for this is a violation. DPA can issue an enforcement action requiring remediation. Affected individuals can lodge complaints and seek compensation for material or non-material damage.

current_finding.title: GDPR data erasure (Art. 17) endpoint requires super-admin key, not data-subject-initiated
current_finding.description: Similar to GDPR-003, the `/admin/gdpr/erase` endpoint (GDPR Article 17 right to erasure) is only accessible to super-admins via X-Admin-Key header. Data subjects have the right to request deletion of their personal data, but this application forces them to contact the photographer/operator instead of providing a self-service mechanism. While the backend logic correctly identifies and deletes data from multiple tables, the access control prevents individuals from exercising this right independently.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Create a public/authenticated endpoint for data erasure requests: POST /gdpr/erasure-request with email and reason. 2. Implement email verification (confirmation link sent to email). 3. If reason is 'withdrawal of consent', allow self-service. If reason is 'right to be forgotten', log the request for manual review by operator. 4. After verification, proceed with gdpr_erase() logic. 5. Provide a response indicating what was deleted and timelines. 6. Log all erasure requests with audit trail.
current_finding.impact: GDPR Article 17 violation. Data subjects have the right to erasure under certain conditions (no longer necessary for purpose, withdrawal of consent, unlawful processing, etc.). Not providing a self-service mechanism violates this right and can result in DPA enforcement and fines.

current_finding.title: Access log retention period unclear and potentially indefinite
current_finding.description: The privacy policy states: 'Pending comments and operational logs are generally retained for about 30 days unless longer review is needed.' However, the access_log table in DynamoDB has a TTL attribute (expires_at) but the code does not set a default expiry time when inserting access log records. This means access logs could remain indefinitely unless the operator manually sets an expiry. GDPR Article 5(1)(e) requires that personal data be kept only as long as necessary. Indefinite or unclear retention violates this principle.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Update access log insertion code to explicitly set expires_at to current_time + 1_year (or operator-configured TTL). 2. Update privacy policy to explicitly state: 'Access logs are retained for 1 year and automatically deleted via DynamoDB TTL.' 3. For sub-admin/demo sessions, confirm default retention is also set and documented. 4. Add configuration option in environment variables to allow customization of retention periods by operator (e.g., LOG_RETENTION_DAYS=365).
current_finding.impact: Indefinite storage of personal data (emails, IPs, activity logs) violates GDPR Article 5(1)(e) storage limitation principle. DPA can issue guidance and enforcement actions. Users can claim that their data is processed longer than necessary.

current_finding.title: Third-party data sharing agreements and DPAs not documented
current_finding.description: The application uses third-party services (Plausible analytics, Resend email, AWS infrastructure) that may process personal data. GDPR Article 6(1) and Chapter III (Articles 44-49) require that data transfers be documented, and data processing agreements (DPAs) must be in place with processors. The privacy policy mentions these services generically but does not provide links to their privacy policies, confirm DPAs are in place, or address how adequacy decisions are handled. Additionally, if these services store data in the US or other non-EEA jurisdictions, Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or other transfer mechanisms must be documented.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Review and confirm DPAs are in place with: AWS (for Lambda, DynamoDB, S3, SES), Plausible (or switch to privacy-friendly alternative like Fathom or Metrical), Resend (if used for transactional emails). 2. Document the legal basis for data transfers (e.g., SCCs, adequacy decision, Binding Corporate Rules). 3. Publish links to third-party privacy policies in your privacy policy. 4. Add section: 'Data processors and transfers: Our data is processed by AWS (Standard Contractual Clauses), Plausible (Privacy Shield or SCCs), and Resend (SCCs).' 5. Maintain a Data Processing Inventory documenting all vendors and their contracts.
current_finding.impact: Without documented DPAs and transfer mechanisms, the application cannot legally transfer EU personal data to US-based services. GDPR Articles 28 and 44-49 require this documentation. DPA enforcement can include orders to cease data transfers and fines.

current_finding.title: Admin API CORS configuration allows all origins (*)
current_finding.description: The API Gateway CORS configuration in terraform/api_gateway.tf explicitly sets: 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *' when responding to admin and auth endpoints. This allows any website to make cross-origin requests to the API from any origin. While the backend still requires authentication (X-Admin-Key or Bearer JWT), the permissive CORS headers increase attack surface: (1) Credential-based attacks can be crafted from any origin. (2) Browser-based CSRF attacks are not protected by CORS. (3) Mistake in authorization logic can be exploited from anywhere. Additionally, the admin handler also sets CORS_ORIGIN from an environment variable with default '*'.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Remove the CORS_ORIGIN environment variable default '*' in lambda/admin/handler.py. Instead, whitelist specific origins: 'ALLOWED_ORIGINS = [os.environ.get("CORS_ORIGIN", "")].filter(bool)' or maintain a hardcoded list. 2. Update the respond() function to check if origin is in whitelist before setting Access-Control-Allow-Origin header. 3. Set CORS_ORIGIN environment variable to the specific gallery domain(s) only. 4. Add X-Requested-With: XMLHttpRequest check for state-changing requests (POST, DELETE). 5. Test that requests from unauthorized origins receive no CORS headers.
current_finding.impact: Overly permissive CORS violates ISO 27001 A.5.15 access control principle. It increases risk of credential-stuffing attacks, CSRF, and data theft. While authentication mitigates some risk, the defense-in-depth principle is violated. Auditors will flag this as a security weakness.

current_finding.title: Access logs and CloudWatch logs include PII (email, IP) without redaction
current_finding.description: Access log records include raw email and IP addresses in CloudWatch logs: 'access_log_table.put_item(Item={..."email": email..."ip": ip...})'. These logs are queryable and may be retained longer than necessary. Additionally, API Gateway logs in CloudWatch include source IP: '"sourceIp": "$context.identity.sourceIp"'. While audit logging is important, GDPR and ISO 27001 A.8.15 require that logs be minimized for PII and retention be limited. Logs containing PII should be subject to the same retention and deletion policies as other personal data.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Update access_log insertion to anonymize or hash email/IP: use hashlib.sha256(email.encode()).hexdigest() instead of raw email. 2. For rate limiting and abuse detection that require IPs, hash the IP as well. 3. Set CloudWatch log retention to match data retention policy (1 year for access logs, 30 days for debug logs). 4. Add a field to distinguish log types: 'type': 'access_attempt', 'failed_auth', 'api_error' to allow filtering sensitive logs. 5. Implement log scrubbing to redact PII from error messages before they're logged.
current_finding.impact: GDPR Article 5 and 6 require that personal data processing be minimized and lawful. Storing PII in audit logs without clear retention policy can violate storage limitation (Art. 5(1)(e)). If logs are breached, it exposes email and IP data. ISO 27001 A.8.15 requires audit logs to be protected and retention-controlled.
current_finding.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.15 — Logging and Audit Trails

current_finding.title: Rate limiting not enforced on GDPR endpoints (/admin/gdpr/search, /admin/gdpr/erase)
current_finding.description: The /admin/gdpr/search and /admin/gdpr/erase endpoints require super-admin key authentication but do not have per-endpoint rate limiting beyond the global WAF (1000 req / 5 min per IP) and API Gateway defaults (100 req/sec, 200 burst). An attacker with the super-admin key (or who obtains it via compromise) could invoke these endpoints repeatedly to enumerate all data subjects in the system or to trigger bulk deletion. Additionally, the ratelimit_table in DynamoDB has server_side_encryption enabled but no point_in_time_recovery, which could allow data loss in case of attack or error.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Add rate limiting to GDPR endpoints: check ratelimit_table before processing, increment counter per email/IP. E.g., 'max 5 access requests per email per hour', 'max 2 erasure requests per email per 24 hours'. 2. Return 429 Too Many Requests if limit exceeded. 3. Enable point_in_time_recovery on ratelimit_table: 'point_in_time_recovery { enabled = true }' in terraform/dynamodb.tf. 4. Log all GDPR requests with detailed audit trail (who, when, what data, outcome). 5. Consider requiring explicit confirmation (second factor) for erasure requests.
current_finding.impact: Without rate limiting on sensitive operations, the system is vulnerable to abuse. An attacker with admin key could exhaust API quotas, enumerate all users, or trigger mass deletions. This violates ISO 27001 A.5.15 (access control) and increases risk of data loss or service disruption.

current_finding.description: The demo_key validation in lambda/admin/handler.py uses a full table scan without pagination, which could allow a slow DoS attack and exposes all demo sessions to be enumerated if demo_key values are guessable.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Create a Global Secondary Index (GSI) on demo_sessions_table with demo_key as hash key: 'global_secondary_index { name = "demo_key-index", hash_key = "demo_key" }'. 2. Query this index instead of full scan: 'demo_sessions_table.query(IndexName="demo_key-index", KeyConditionExpression=Key("demo_key").eq(key))'. 3. Set Limit=1 on the query since demo_key should be unique.
current_finding.impact: Inefficient data access patterns consume resources and could be exploited for DoS. This violates ISO 27001 A.5.15 (access control) principle of minimizing resource consumption for security operations.
current_finding.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.25 — Input Validation and Sanitization

current_finding.title: CloudFront signed URLs use SHA1 for signing (deprecated cryptographic hash)
current_finding.description: CloudFront signed URL generation uses SHA1 (PKCS#1 v1.5 padding) to sign URLs: 'signature = private_key.sign(policy.encode(), padding.PKCS1v15(), hashes.SHA1())'. SHA1 is deprecated and no longer recommended for cryptographic purposes due to collision vulnerabilities. While CloudFront still supports SHA1 and collisions are difficult to produce in this specific context (signing structured policy documents), using a deprecated algorithm violates ISO 27001 A.8.3 cryptographic controls best practice. This is also noted in pyproject.toml as an exception: 'S303 — SHA1 required by CloudFront signed URLs'.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Monitor CloudFront and AWS documentation for support of SHA256 signing (may require changes to CloudFront key pair type or region support). 2. If SHA256 becomes available, migrate to it: 'hashes.SHA256()' instead of 'hashes.SHA1()'. 3. For now, document this as a 'known limitation' due to CloudFront API constraints and confirm with AWS that SHA1 is secure enough for this use case. 4. Add a note to security.astro or security documentation explaining the trade-off.
current_finding.impact: Low immediate risk since CloudFront's implementation is robust and collision attacks are impractical for this use case. However, continued use of SHA1 violates ISO 27001 best practices and modern cryptographic standards. EU and international bodies recommend phasing out SHA1 entirely. Auditors will flag this as a technical debt.

current_finding.title: No explicit requirement for TLS 1.3 or minimum version enforcement
current_finding.description: While HSTS and signed URLs enforce HTTPS, there is no explicit minimum TLS version requirement in the CloudFront or API Gateway configuration. The default AWS behavior should be TLS 1.2+, but best practice (ISO 27001 A.8.24) is to explicitly require TLS 1.3 or at minimum TLS 1.2, and disable older versions. Additionally, no explicit mention of certificate pinning for mobile clients (if any).
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. In CloudFront distribution resource, add: 'minimum_protocol_version = "TLSv1.2_2021-06"' (or TLSv1.3 if available). 2. In API Gateway, confirm default TLS version is 1.2+ via AWS documentation or API. 3. Consider adding Security-Headers HPKP (Public Key Pinning) header if mobile clients are used. 4. Test with nmap or testssl.sh to confirm TLS 1.0/1.1 are disabled.
current_finding.impact: Low risk due to AWS defaults being secure. However, explicitly configuring minimum TLS version demonstrates compliance with ISO 27001 and defense-in-depth. Without explicit configuration, any AWS default change could reduce security.

current_finding.title: No comprehensive audit trail for administrator actions
current_finding.description: The admin Lambda logs some operations (create gallery, delete gallery, GDPR actions) to CloudWatch, but there is no centralized audit trail that explicitly tracks all admin actions with sufficient detail (who, what, when, where, result). For example, gallery updates, photo approval/rejection, comment moderation, and team member creation are not explicitly logged with user identity and timestamp.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Create a dedicated audit_log function that logs all admin actions: 'def audit_log(action, resource_id, user_id, result, details={})'. 2. Call this function for: create/update/delete gallery, approve/reject photo, moderate comment, create/modify sub-admin, generate tokens. 3. Store audit logs in a separate immutable table or CloudWatch log group with long retention (3-7 years). 4. Include fields: timestamp, action, resource_id, user_id, IP, result (success/failure), details. 5. Implement log aggregation and alerting for suspicious patterns (e.g., multiple failed auth attempts, mass deletions).
current_finding.impact: Low immediate risk, but lack of comprehensive audit trail weakens compliance posture. ISO 27001 A.8.15 requires that all security-relevant events be logged. If a data breach occurs, incomplete audit logs hinder incident investigation and may violate GDPR Article 33 breach notification requirements.

current_finding.title: No documented incident response or breach notification procedure
current_finding.description: There is no documented incident response plan, breach notification procedure, or runbook for handling security incidents. The security.astro page mentions a security contact email but does not provide procedures for how incidents are triaged, investigated, or how users are notified in case of a breach. GDPR Article 33 requires notification to supervisory authorities within 72 hours of discovering a personal data breach.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Create docs/INCIDENT-RESPONSE.md documenting: (a) How security issues are reported (email, responsible person). (b) Initial triage and severity assessment. (c) Investigation steps and forensic procedures. (d) Notification timelines (internal: immediate, DPA: 72 hours, users: without undue delay). (e) Communication templates for affected users. (f) Remediation and recovery steps. 2. Create docs/BREACH-NOTIFICATION.md with breach notification template that includes: nature of breach, categories of personal data, likely consequences, measures taken to mitigate. 3. Set up CloudWatch alarms for suspicious activities (high rate of deletions, auth failures, WAF blocks) and route them to the security team via SNS.
current_finding.impact: GDPR Article 33 requires breach notification within 72 hours. Without a documented procedure, the likelihood of timely notification is reduced. This can result in regulatory fines and legal liability. ISO 27001 A.5.24 requires incident response planning.

current_finding.title: No Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) documented
current_finding.description: GDPR Article 35 requires that a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) be conducted for processing that is 'likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons'. Analogify processes personal photos, email addresses, IP addresses, and activity data, and may involve automated processing (e.g., photo analysis by ChromaHaus). This is exactly the type of processing that typically requires a DPIA. There is no evidence of a DPIA being conducted or documented.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Conduct a DPIA following the EDPB guidelines (EDPB Template or ISO/IEC 27550). 2. Document processing activities: (a) Identification of data controller/processor. (b) Purposes of processing. (c) Legitimate interest assessment (if applicable). (d) Categories of data subjects and personal data. (e) Risk assessment: identify risks to individuals' rights and freedoms. (f) Mitigation measures (technical and organizational). (g) Necessity and proportionality assessment. 3. Store the DPIA in docs/DPIA.md and reference it in the privacy policy. 4. If risks are identified as high, consult with supervisory authority per Article 36.
current_finding.impact: GDPR Article 35 violation. Failure to conduct a DPIA when required can result in regulatory guidance, enforcement orders, and fines (€10-20M or up to 4% of global turnover). A DPIA is a demonstration of compliance and helps identify and mitigate risks.
current_finding.regulation: GDPR Art. 35 — Data Protection Impact Assessment

current_finding.title: No Data Protection Officer (DPO) role or contact information
current_finding.description: GDPR Article 37 requires certain organizations to designate a Data Protection Officer (DPO). While Analogify is likely a small organization and may be exempt, the privacy policy does not clarify whether a DPO is designated or who the responsible person is for privacy inquiries. This creates ambiguity for individuals trying to exercise their rights or contact the organization about privacy concerns.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Determine if Analogify meets DPO requirement criteria under GDPR Article 37(1) (public authority, core activity is systematic monitoring, large-scale processing of special categories). 2. If exempt, add to privacy policy: 'A Data Protection Officer is not required under GDPR Article 37. Privacy inquiries can be directed to: cody@codyjo.com'. 3. If DPO is required or voluntarily designated, add to privacy policy: 'Our Data Protection Officer is [Name] ([email])' and ensure DPO contact is publicly available.
current_finding.impact: Low risk if organization is exempt from DPO requirement (fewer than 250 employees, non-systematic monitoring, not processing special categories at scale). However, absence of clarity creates regulatory risk and user confusion. Auditors may flag this as incomplete governance.

current_finding.title: Age verification not applicable to this project
current_finding.description: Analogify Studio is a B2B photographer gallery delivery platform. It does not serve content directly to end consumers, nor does it host adult content, social media functionality, or content restricted by age. The platform is designed for photographers to deliver client galleries, which are access-controlled and requires gallery owner authentication. Individual photographers are responsible for compliance with age verification laws if they serve minors. The platform itself does not need to implement age verification gates.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: No action required. However, if in future the platform adds: (1) a public gallery directory with uncontrolled access, (2) social media features, (3) age-restricted content delivery, or (4) consumer-facing apps (Analogify Zen backend), age verification may become applicable.
current_finding.impact: No legal risk. Age verification requirements do not apply to this project type.
current_finding.regulation: US State Laws (Louisiana, Utah, Texas, Virginia, Arkansas, Montana, California), UK Online Safety Act

current_finding.title: Secrets and API keys stored in plaintext in configuration files
current_finding.description: The backoffice.yaml configuration file (intentionally gitignored) stores sensitive values in plaintext: AWS access/secret keys, CloudFront distribution IDs, API keys, and database credentials. If this file is compromised, an attacker gains full access to AWS infrastructure, dashboards, and potentially target repositories. No encryption at rest is applied to configuration data.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Integrate AWS Secrets Manager or HashiCorp Vault for secrets storage. 2. Modify backoffice/config.py to fetch sensitive values from the secrets manager at startup. 3. Use environment variable references in backoffice.yaml for sensitive fields (e.g., api_key: "${API_KEY}"). 4. Document the secrets management approach in setup instructions. 5. Add pre-commit hook to prevent accidental commits of plaintext secrets.
current_finding.impact: If configuration is compromised, unauthorized access to audit findings and target infrastructure violates ISO 27001 control A.8.2 and A.8.24 requirements. Potential exposure of AWS credentials could lead to infrastructure compromise, data theft, and financial liability (AWS abuse).

current_finding.title: No enforcement of HTTPS/TLS for API server
current_finding.description: The api_server.py and server.py use Python's http.server (HTTP only). There is no built-in TLS/SSL support. If deployed directly (not behind a reverse proxy with TLS termination), all API traffic is sent in plaintext, exposing API keys, findings data, and scan requests.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Document that the API server MUST be deployed behind a reverse proxy (nginx, Apache, AWS ALB) with TLS termination. 2. Alternatively, add ssl.wrap_socket() context to api_server.py if direct HTTPS is needed. 3. Add a startup check that logs a WARNING if api_key is configured but no reverse proxy is detected. 4. Update deployment documentation to include HTTPS-required notation. 5. Add security headers (HSTS, CSP) in the reverse proxy configuration.
current_finding.impact: API keys and audit findings transmitted over plaintext HTTP violate ISO 27001 A.8.24 encryption in transit requirements. Man-in-the-middle attacks could intercept API keys or manipulate findings.

current_finding.title: No rate limiting on API endpoints
current_finding.description: The API server (api_server.py) accepts requests without rate limiting. An attacker can brute-force API keys, enumerate scan endpoints, or trigger denial-of-service by spawning unlimited concurrent agent jobs (each consuming CPU/memory).
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Implement per-IP rate limiting using a library like python-ratelimit or Flask-Limiter (even though this is a raw http.server). 2. Add a simple in-memory token bucket or sliding window counter keyed by request origin. 3. Return HTTP 429 (Too Many Requests) when rate limit is exceeded. 4. Recommend deploying behind a reverse proxy with rate limiting (nginx limit_req, AWS WAF). 5. Document rate limit thresholds in the API documentation.
current_finding.impact: Lack of rate limiting enables brute-force attacks against API keys and denial-of-service attacks, violating ISO 27001 A.8.2 authentication controls and A.8.1 access control principles.
current_finding.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.2 (User Authentication Management) - Rate Limiting

current_finding.title: Insufficient audit logging of security-relevant events
current_finding.description: While backoffice/log_config.py provides structured logging, security-relevant events are not comprehensively logged: failed API authentication attempts are not explicitly logged, API key usage is not tracked, unauthorized endpoint access is not recorded, and data access patterns are not logged. Logs may also contain exception stacktraces with sensitive information (file paths, internal structure).
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Add explicit logging for authentication failures: log failed API key attempts with source IP and timestamp. 2. Log all API requests with method, path, source IP, API key ID (not value), status code, and response size. 3. Add separate audit log file (audit.log) with immutable append-only semantics. 4. Sanitize exception logs to avoid exposing file paths, config values, or internal structure. 5. Implement log retention and rotation policies.
current_finding.impact: Lack of comprehensive audit logs prevents detection of unauthorized access, API key compromise, or data tampering. Violates ISO 27001 A.8.15/A.8.16 requirements for logging and monitoring.

current_finding.title: No API key rotation mechanism
current_finding.description: API keys are stored in backoffice.yaml and read at startup. There is no rotation schedule, no expiration policy, and no mechanism to revoke compromised keys. If a key is leaked, the only remedy is manual file editing and service restart.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Implement API key rotation: support multiple active keys with expiration dates. 2. Move keys to a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). 3. Add --rotate-key command to CLI to generate new keys. 4. Log all key rotations and revocations. 5. Document a key rotation policy (e.g., rotate every 90 days).
current_finding.impact: Lack of key rotation increases the window of exposure if a key is compromised. Violates ISO 27001 A.8.2 principles of timely access control updates.

current_finding.title: Missing Content-Security-Policy and security headers on dashboard
current_finding.description: Dashboard HTML files (e.g., dashboard/index.html) include a basic CSP header but it allows unsafe-inline for scripts and styles. This permits XSS attacks if any user-controlled data (e.g., findings from audited repos) is displayed without proper escaping. Additionally, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy, and cache-control headers are missing.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Upgrade CSP to remove 'unsafe-inline' for scripts: use nonce-based or hash-based script execution. 2. Move inline styles to external stylesheets. 3. Add X-Frame-Options: DENY to prevent clickjacking. 4. Add X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff to prevent MIME sniffing. 5. Add Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin. 6. Add Permissions-Policy to disable sensitive browser features (geolocation, camera, microphone). 7. For sensitive pages, add Cache-Control: no-store, private.
current_finding.impact: XSS vulnerabilities via unsafe CSP allow attackers to steal API keys, session cookies, or inject malicious content into dashboards. Violates ISO 27001 A.8.9 security headers and A.8.25 output encoding principles.

current_finding.title: Insufficient input validation on TARGET parameter in agent scripts
current_finding.description: The api_server.py and shell scripts accept a 'target' parameter from the request body. While Python's subprocess.run() with a list avoids shell injection, the target path is not validated against a whitelist. An attacker could potentially specify an arbitrary filesystem path (e.g., /etc/passwd) as a target, causing unexpected behavior.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Validate that the supplied target name matches one of the configured targets in backoffice.yaml. 2. Reject any target path that is not explicitly configured. 3. Add an explicit whitelist check before calling run_agent(). 4. Log rejected scan attempts with the supplied target and source IP.
current_finding.impact: Arbitrary path traversal could expose audit findings on unintended repos or trigger scans on sensitive directories, violating ISO 27001 A.8.26 input validation controls.

current_finding.title: CORS configuration could be overly permissive if misconfigured
current_finding.description: api_server.py supports wildcard CORS origin ('*' in allowed_origins). While the default config (backoffice.example.yaml) specifies legitimate origins, a misconfigured deployment could expose the API to requests from any origin, allowing cross-site request forgery attacks.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Update backoffice/api_server.py to reject '*' as an allowed origin (comment out or remove the wildcard check). 2. Document that allowed_origins must be an explicit list of trusted domains. 3. Add a validation check during config loading to reject wildcard origins and log a warning. 4. Recommend deploying with specific, domain-restricted CORS origins only.
current_finding.impact: Overly permissive CORS enables cross-site request forgery (CSRF) attacks where a malicious website triggers requests to the back-office API on behalf of an authenticated user. Violates ISO 27001 A.8.9 access control principles.

current_finding.title: No explicit request body size limits
current_finding.description: The api_server.py and server.py do not set maximum request body sizes. An attacker could send arbitrarily large POST payloads to exhaust server memory or trigger denial-of-service conditions.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Set a maximum request body size (e.g., 10MB). 2. In _read_body(), check if Content-Length > MAX_BODY_SIZE and return 413 Payload Too Large. 3. Document the maximum payload size in API documentation. 4. Recommend deploying behind a reverse proxy (nginx, ALB) with request size limits enforced at the perimeter.
current_finding.impact: Lack of request size limits enables denial-of-service attacks that consume memory and CPU, violating ISO 27001 A.8.3 network security controls.

current_finding.title: Registration consent is required in the UI but not recorded server-side
current_finding.description: The registration form requires a privacy-policy checkbox, but the submitted payload contains only name, email, password, and preferredVersion, and the backend profile record stores no consent timestamp, policy version, or consent scope. That leaves the service unable to evidence that consent was freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add explicit consent fields to the register payload, store consent scope, timestamp, and policy/version identifiers in DynamoDB, and expose withdrawal/preference management for any optional processing tied to consent.
current_finding.impact: If Selah relies on consent for account creation disclosures, analytics, or other optional processing, it cannot demonstrate valid consent during a regulator inquiry or user complaint, creating exposure to corrective orders and administrative fines.

current_finding.title: Third-party analytics loads before any consent or preference flow
current_finding.description: The root layout injects Plausible analytics for every visitor as soon as the app loads. The repository contains no consent banner, analytics preference center, or conditional loading path tied to user choice. Even for cookieless analytics, this creates a GDPR transparency and lawful-basis gap unless the operator has documented and disclosed a defensible alternative basis outside the codebase.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Implement a consent/preferences layer for analytics and gate Plausible script injection on that preference, or remove third-party analytics for regulated users and update privacy disclosures accordingly.
current_finding.impact: Processing analytics data for EEA users without a demonstrable lawful basis or clear opt-out/consent workflow can trigger complaints, orders to stop processing, and regulator scrutiny over third-party transfers and transparency.

current_finding.title: The backend supports account deletion, but the app exposes no user-facing erasure flow
current_finding.description: The API provides an authenticated DELETE /api/account route that removes account data, but the Settings page exposes sign-out, backup, import/export, and password-change controls only. There is no in-product delete-account or erase-my-data path, which makes the right to erasure materially harder to exercise.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add a delete-account flow in Settings that requires password re-entry, calls DELETE /api/account, clears local data, and confirms what data will be removed.
current_finding.impact: Users may be unable to exercise Article 17 rights without manual support intervention, increasing complaint risk and making statutory response timelines harder to meet.

current_finding.title: No retention limit is applied to persisted account data
current_finding.description: The DynamoDB table supports TTL only through the expiresAt attribute, but user profile, study, journal, bookmark, and daily records are written without any retention field. The repo contains no cleanup job or retention-policy implementation for personal data stored in the account backend.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Define retention periods by data type, store expiry metadata where appropriate, and add scheduled purge logic or lifecycle jobs. This requires business and legal input before code changes.
current_finding.impact: Indefinite retention of personal data without a documented necessity basis can violate the storage-limitation principle and widen exposure if a breach or DSAR occurs.

current_finding.title: Bearer tokens and user profile data are persisted in localStorage
current_finding.description: Authentication tokens, user profile data, and cached encryption metadata are stored in browser localStorage. Because localStorage is readable by any injected JavaScript, a future XSS defect or compromised third-party script would expose active sessions and user identifiers. This is weaker than an HttpOnly session-cookie model for a production consumer app processing account and journal data.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Move session handling to Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite cookies or another server-managed session mechanism, and keep only strictly necessary non-sensitive state in web storage.
current_finding.impact: Session theft can lead to unauthorized access to personal data, triggering incident-response obligations, account compromise, and possible breach-notification exposure under both ISO-aligned controls and privacy law.

current_finding.title: Account backup uploads journal content in plaintext when the encryption key is unavailable
current_finding.description: The account-backup path encrypts journals only when a master key is present. If the user is signed in but the in-memory key is unavailable, the backup routine sends the raw journal entry to the API, and the backend stores plaintext content. That defeats the stated zero-knowledge journal design for a common recovery state.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Block journal backup until the journal key is unlocked, or split backup so journals are skipped with a clear warning unless encryption is active. On the backend, reject plaintext journal writes for accounts that have encryption enabled.
current_finding.impact: Sensitive spiritual reflections and prayer-journal content may be stored unencrypted in the backend, increasing breach impact and undermining security assurances made to users.

current_finding.title: Content Security Policy allows unsafe inline and eval execution
current_finding.description: The CloudFront security headers policy sets a CSP, but it permits both 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval' in script-src. Those exceptions materially weaken XSS defenses, especially because the frontend also stores bearer tokens in browser storage and loads third-party scripts.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Remove unsafe-inline and unsafe-eval, migrate any inline bootstrapping to nonce- or hash-based scripts, and confirm the build/runtime path works without eval-dependent tooling in production.
current_finding.impact: A weaker CSP increases the likelihood that an injection flaw becomes a real account-compromise incident, which raises security and breach-response risk.

current_finding.title: Privacy policy missing data retention period, DPO contact, and specific lawful basis
current_finding.description: The privacy policy at /privacy.astro discloses data collection and third parties but omits: (1) specific retention period for contact form data, (2) Data Protection Officer contact information, (3) explicit lawful basis per processing activity (Article 6), (4) breach notification procedures. GDPR Articles 13-14 require complete transparency at the point of data collection.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Expand privacy policy to include: (1) 'Contact form data is retained for [30-90] days or until inquiry is resolved, whichever is longer, then deleted.' (2) 'We process contact data on the basis of [consent given via checkbox / legitimate interest in responding to inquiries].' (3) DPO contact if applicable, or clarify no separate DPO is appointed. (4) 'Suspected breaches are reported to [supervisory authority] within 72 hours. Contact privacy@codyjo.com for security incidents.'
