current_finding.impact: Incomplete privacy notices violate GDPR Articles 13-14 and can trigger enforcement fines up to €20M or 4% of global turnover. Data subjects can claim damages for non-transparent processing.

current_finding.title: No documented mechanism for users to exercise data subject rights (access, deletion, portability)
current_finding.description: Privacy policy states users can request access, correction, or deletion, but provides no email address, contact form, or API endpoint for submitting such requests. GDPR Articles 15-22 require controllers to establish a practical mechanism for data subjects to exercise their rights.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Add to privacy policy: 'To exercise your data subject rights, email privacy@codyjo.com with your request. Requests for access will return contact form submissions, timestamps, and consent records within 30 days. Requests for deletion will remove all personal data unless we have a legal obligation to retain it.'
current_finding.impact: GDPR requires controllers to respond to access requests within 30 days. Lack of a documented mechanism may be interpreted as failure to facilitate rights exercise. Fines up to €10M or 2% of turnover can apply.
current_finding.regulation: GDPR Art. 15-22 — Data Subject Rights Mechanism

current_finding.title: No documented Data Processing Agreement (DPA) with Plausible Analytics processor
current_finding.description: Privacy policy discloses Plausible but does not confirm a signed Data Processing Agreement. GDPR Article 28 requires a DPA when a processor handles personal data on behalf of the controller. Plausible typically offers a standard DPA, but lack of documented agreement creates compliance risk.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Verify Plausible DPA is in place (typically included in their standard terms). Update privacy policy to state: 'Plausible is our data processor under a Data Processing Agreement that complies with GDPR Article 28. Plausible does not process personal data for any purpose other than providing analytics services to us.'
current_finding.impact: Absence of documented DPA is a direct GDPR Article 28 violation. Fines up to €10M or 2% of turnover apply. If Plausible's default terms include a DPA, lack of acknowledgment still creates audit risk.
current_finding.regulation: GDPR Art. 28 — Data Processing Agreement for Plausible

current_finding.title: No documented legal mechanism for US data transfers (Schrems II compliance)
current_finding.description: Contact form data is sent to AWS Lambda in us-west-2. Privacy policy mentions US hosting but does not reference Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs), adequacy decisions, or equivalent safeguards required by GDPR Articles 44-49. Post-Schrems II, inadequate transfer mechanisms expose the controller to enforcement action.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Confirm AWS Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) are in place via AWS Data Processing Addendum. Update privacy policy: 'Personal data is transferred to the United States under Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) that ensure adequate safeguards. You may request details of the transfer mechanism by contacting privacy@codyjo.com.'
current_finding.impact: Without documented legal transfer mechanisms, EU personal data transfer to the US violates GDPR. Recent Schrems II ruling emphasizes strict requirements. Fines up to €20M or 4% of turnover apply. Irish DPC has taken enforcement action on US transfer gaps.

current_finding.title: No clear mechanism for users to withdraw contact form consent
current_finding.description: Contact form requires consent checkbox but privacy policy does not explain how to withdraw consent or what happens to data upon withdrawal. GDPR Article 7(3) requires withdrawal to be as easy as giving consent.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Update privacy policy to include: 'You can withdraw your consent at any time by contacting privacy@codyjo.com. Upon withdrawal, we will delete your contact form data unless we have a legal obligation to retain it.'
current_finding.impact: GDPR Art. 7(3) requires transparent withdrawal mechanism. Lack of clarity may trigger regulator complaints.

current_finding.title: CSP header contains 'unsafe-inline' for scripts, weakening XSS protection
current_finding.description: Content-Security-Policy header in Base.astro line 66 includes script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' https://plausible.io. The 'unsafe-inline' directive defeats XSS protection by allowing arbitrary inline script execution without nonce or hash validation. This significantly weakens browser-enforced security for a site collecting contact data.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Remove 'unsafe-inline' from script-src and use nonces: (1) Add a nonce to Plausible script tag (line 123). (2) Astro should support automatic nonce generation. (3) Update CSP to: script-src 'self' 'nonce-[generated]' https://plausible.io;. (4) Move inline Plausible init code to external file or apply nonce.
current_finding.impact: XSS vulnerabilities on a form collecting contact data could lead to credential theft or data exfiltration, violating GDPR Article 32 (security measures) and ISO 27001 controls. Demonstrated XSS leading to breach could trigger enforcement.

current_finding.title: Missing HTTP security headers: X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy
current_finding.description: Site does not explicitly configure several important HTTP security headers to prevent clickjacking, MIME-sniffing, referrer leakage, and browser feature abuse. While CloudFront may apply defaults, relying on implicit CDN behavior rather than explicit configuration is a compliance gap.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Configure CloudFront response headers policy or Astro middleware to set: (1) X-Frame-Options: DENY (prevent clickjacking). (2) X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff (prevent MIME-sniffing). (3) Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin (limit referrer leakage). (4) Permissions-Policy: camera=(), microphone=(), geolocation=() (restrict browser features).
current_finding.impact: Missing security headers increase attack surface for clickjacking and data leakage. If these enable a breach, GDPR Article 32 and ISO 27001 controls are undermined.

current_finding.title: Contact form client-side validation only; server-side validation not visible
current_finding.description: Contact form implements client-side validation (email regex, required fields, length limits) but the AWS Lambda backend code is not in the repository. Server-side validation cannot be verified, creating risk of malformed or injection-attack payloads being stored.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Verify that the AWS Lambda function implements server-side validation: (1) Email format (robust regex or validation library). (2) Maximum length checks. (3) Required field validation. (4) Character restrictions (reject control characters, unusual Unicode). (5) XSS prevention (escape HTML entities before storage). Return 400/422 if validation fails.
current_finding.impact: Client-side validation can be bypassed. Without confirmed server-side validation, injection attacks or malformed data could compromise data integrity or enable XSS if data is later reflected.

current_finding.title: Contact API endpoint lacks documented authentication or authorization
current_finding.description: Contact form submits to AWS API Gateway endpoint with no visible authentication (no API key, bearer token, or OAuth). While honeypot and rate-limiting provide basic protection, a public endpoint without authentication can be abused for spam, DoS, or malicious data injection.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Implement API authentication: (1) Add API key validation in Lambda (check x-api-key header against environment variable). (2) Or use AWS API Gateway Lambda authorizer with IAM role. (3) Verify CORS is restricted to https://www.codyjo.com origin. (4) Confirm 429 rate-limiting is in place (already implemented per code).
current_finding.impact: Unprotected API endpoints risk abuse, spam flooding, or data injection. If attackers inject malicious content or cause service degradation, it undermines ISO 27001 A.8.2 access controls and availability.

current_finding.title: No documented retention period for server and CDN logs
current_finding.description: Privacy policy mentions 'server-side and CDN request logs' but does not specify retention duration. Log retention policy is essential for audit trails and incident investigation per ISO 27001 A.5.11.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Update privacy policy: 'Server and CDN logs are retained for 90 days in accordance with AWS CloudFront default retention and used for security investigation and troubleshooting. Logs are not shared with third parties unless required by law.'
current_finding.impact: Lack of documented log retention complicates breach investigation and incident response. GDPR Article 32 and ISO 27001 A.5.11 expect documented audit logging.

current_finding.title: CSP allows overly broad AWS API Gateway endpoint pattern
current_finding.description: CSP includes connect-src https://*.execute-api.us-west-2.amazonaws.com, allowing connections to any API Gateway endpoint in the region. This is broader than necessary and could allow requests to unintended endpoints if an attacker compromises the site's JavaScript context.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Restrict CSP to the specific API endpoint: connect-src 'self' https://ldnybm21m6.execute-api.us-west-2.amazonaws.com https://plausible.io; If the endpoint domain is managed externally, document why the wildcard is necessary.
current_finding.impact: Overly permissive CSP reduces protection value. Low risk for a static portfolio site but represents unnecessary attack surface.

current_finding.title: No documented Record of Processing Activities (RoPA)
current_finding.description: GDPR Article 30 requires controllers to maintain a Record of Processing Activities. While small sites may have simple records, there is no evidence of a formal RoPA document in the repository or accompanying documentation.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Create a simple Record of Processing Activities (internal document, not public) listing: (1) Contact form: name/email/message, purpose: respond to inquiry, retention: 30-90 days, basis: consent, no sharing. (2) Plausible analytics: aggregated usage, purpose: understand traffic, retention: per Plausible policy, basis: consent or legitimate interest, processor: Plausible.
current_finding.impact: Lack of formal RoPA does not automatically violate GDPR, but it weakens accountability and makes audits harder. Regulators expect to see a RoPA during enforcement actions.

current_finding.title: Missing privacy policy and data processing disclosure
current_finding.description: The pe-bootstrap web UI collects personal data (requestor name, scenario description, console password, deployment preferences) but provides no privacy policy, data processing notice, or transparency about how data is handled. Users are not informed of their data subject rights, retention periods, or legal basis for processing. A privacy policy is required by GDPR Art. 13 (pre-collection disclosure) and Art. 14 (post-collection disclosure if data wasn't directly collected from the data subject).
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Create a privacy policy page (e.g., frontend/web/privacy.html) that discloses: (a) controller identity and contact details, (b) purposes of processing (infrastructure deployment, audit logging), (c) legal basis (Art. 6 — likely legitimate interest or contract), (d) categories of personal data collected, (e) retention periods, (f) data subject rights and how to exercise them, (g) recipients if data is shared. 2. Link the privacy policy from the UI (footer or settings). 3. Add a notice on the deployment form explaining data handling. 4. Update API documentation with data handling expectations.
current_finding.impact: GDPR violations can result in administrative fines up to €20 million or 4% of annual global turnover, whichever is higher. Failure to provide privacy notices violates Art. 13–14 and could trigger supervisory authority enforcement action.

current_finding.title: No documented lawful basis for personal data processing
current_finding.description: The system collects and processes personal data (requestor name, email from Google OAuth, scenario descriptions, console password) without establishing or documenting a lawful basis under GDPR Art. 6. The system does not explicitly ask for consent, disclose legitimate interests, or establish a contractual relationship. Without a clear lawful basis, the processing is unlawful.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Determine the lawful basis for each processing activity: Likely candidates are (a) Art. 6(1)(b) if pe-bootstrap is part of an employment or service contract, (b) Art. 6(1)(f) legitimate interests (infrastructure management, security, operational need). 2. Document the basis clearly in the privacy policy. 3. If using legitimate interests, conduct and document a Legitimate Interest Assessment (LIA) showing that interests are not overridden by individual rights. 4. Update the terms of service or user agreement to reference the lawful basis. 5. For Google OAuth, clarify in the privacy policy that you are relying on consent (Art. 7) or another basis, and ensure users can withdraw consent.
current_finding.impact: Processing personal data without a lawful basis is a material GDPR violation. Supervisory authorities can issue cease-and-desist orders and impose significant fines (up to €20 million or 4% of turnover).

current_finding.title: No mechanism for data subject access requests
current_finding.description: Users/requestors have no way to request and receive a copy of their personal data held by the system. GDPR Art. 15 grants data subjects the right to obtain a copy of their data within 30 days. The system stores requestor names, scenario descriptions, deployment metadata, and audit trails in GCS but provides no way for users to export or retrieve this data.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Implement a /api/user/data endpoint that returns all personal data associated with the authenticated user (deployment metadata, audit log entries, etc.). 2. Return data in a structured, machine-readable format (JSON or CSV). 3. Add a button in the web UI (e.g., under Settings or Account) that allows users to download their data. 4. Document the 30-day SLA for responding to requests.
current_finding.impact: Failure to respond to data subject access requests within 30 days violates GDPR Art. 15 and can result in enforcement orders and fines.

current_finding.title: No mechanism for data deletion or erasure
current_finding.description: While users can destroy their deployments, the system does not provide a way to delete the associated personal data (requestor metadata, audit trails, deployment records) from the system. GDPR Art. 17 grants the right to erasure (except where data must be retained for legal/contractual reasons). Metadata remains in GCS indefinitely after deployment destruction.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Implement a delete endpoint /api/deployments/{deployment_id}/delete that (a) verifies the authenticated user owns the deployment, (b) deletes metadata, outputs, and audit logs from GCS, (c) returns a confirmation. 2. Create a retention policy: offer a 30-day grace period before hard deletion, then purge all data. 3. Add a 'delete my data' option in the web UI. 4. Document the retention period in the privacy policy.
current_finding.impact: Failure to honor erasure requests violates GDPR Art. 17 and can result in enforcement orders and fines. Prolonged retention of personal data without justification violates Art. 5 (storage limitation).

current_finding.title: No documented data retention policy
current_finding.description: The system collects and stores personal data (requestor metadata, audit trails, deployment outputs) with no documented retention policy or automatic cleanup mechanism. GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) requires that personal data be kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than necessary. The system appears to retain deployment metadata indefinitely.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Define a data retention policy: (a) Deployment metadata: retain for 90 days post-deployment-destruction, then delete. (b) Audit trails: retain for 1 year for compliance/security purposes, then delete. 2. Implement automated deletion via GCP Cloud Scheduler + Cloud Functions or GCS lifecycle rules. 3. Document the retention policy in the privacy policy and CLAUDE.md. 4. Add a /api/user/data-retention endpoint that discloses the retention schedule to users.
current_finding.impact: Indefinite retention of personal data violates the storage limitation principle (GDPR Art. 5(1)(e)) and can result in enforcement action. Regulators expect documented, justified retention periods.
current_finding.regulation: GDPR Art. 5(1)(e), Art. 25 (Storage Limitation & Data Protection by Design)

current_finding.title: Missing security headers and TLS enforcement
current_finding.description: The web UI does not implement critical HTTP security headers (Content-Security-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy) that are required by GDPR Art. 32 (security measures commensurate with the level of risk). Absence of these headers increases vulnerability to XSS, clickjacking, and data interception.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Add security headers to Flask responses via middleware or after_request hook: (a) Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload (b) Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' https://accounts.google.com; img-src 'self' data: (c) X-Frame-Options: DENY (d) X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff (e) Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin (f) Cache-Control: private, no-store for sensitive endpoints. 2. Restrict CORS to specific origins (not wildcard). 3. Enforce HTTPS redirects.
current_finding.impact: GDPR Art. 32 requires appropriate technical and organizational measures, including encryption and integrity verification. Missing security headers violate this requirement and could be cited in breach notifications. Attackers could intercept data or perform XSS attacks, exposing personal data.

current_finding.title: Overly permissive CORS configuration
current_finding.description: CORS is enabled with no explicit origin restrictions (CORS(app) enables all origins). This allows any website to make requests to the API on behalf of authenticated users, increasing the risk of cross-site request forgery (CSRF) and unauthorized access if an attacker can trick a user into visiting a malicious site while logged in.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Replace CORS(app) with explicit origin whitelisting: from flask_cors import CORS; CORS(app, resources={'/api/*': {'origins': ['https://pe-bootstrap.example.com']}}) 2. Identify the frontend domain(s) that will call the API and whitelist only those. 3. Set credentials=True and allow_headers=['Authorization', 'Content-Type'].
current_finding.impact: Overly permissive CORS violates GDPR Art. 32 security requirements. A breach via CORS misconfiguration could expose deployment metadata and audit logs, triggering breach notification obligations.

current_finding.title: No mechanism to correct inaccurate personal data
current_finding.description: Users cannot correct inaccurate requestor names or scenario descriptions after deployment creation. GDPR Art. 16 grants the right to rectification of inaccurate data. The system should allow users to update these fields.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Add a PATCH /api/deployments/{deployment_id} endpoint that allows users to update: scenario, requestor name. 2. Verify user ownership of the deployment before allowing updates. 3. Log all changes in the audit trail. 4. Add an edit button in the web UI (e.g., in the deployment details page).
current_finding.impact: Failure to provide a way to correct inaccurate personal data violates GDPR Art. 16 and contributes to inaccuracy violations under Art. 5(1)(d).

current_finding.title: Audit logs stored without integrity protection
current_finding.description: Audit logs are appended to JSON files in GCS (append_audit_event, line 436) but there is no cryptographic proof of integrity or tamper-detection. An attacker with GCS write access could modify logs to hide their actions. ISO 27001 A.8.15 requires audit logs to be protected against unauthorized access and modification.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Migrate audit logging to Google Cloud Logging (Stackdriver) instead of JSON files in GCS. Cloud Logging provides immutable, tamper-proof audit trails. 2. Configure log retention (e.g., 90 days for operational logs, 1 year for security-relevant logs) via log sinks. 3. Set up log analysis/alerting for suspicious events (multiple failed auth attempts, unauthorized API calls). 4. Ensure logs are accessible to security and compliance teams but not to end users.
current_finding.impact: Lack of log integrity violates ISO 27001 A.8.15 and GDPR Art. 32 requirements for secure processing. In the event of a breach, logs may be considered unreliable evidence, hampering breach investigation and regulatory response.

current_finding.title: Session tokens stored in browser sessionStorage (sensitive)
current_finding.description: Authentication tokens (passphrase or Google ID token) are stored in browser sessionStorage (app.js line 82, 34), which is readable by any JavaScript on the page. While sessionStorage is cleared on browser close (better than localStorage), it is still vulnerable to XSS attacks. A malicious script can read sessionStorage and steal the token.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Store authentication tokens in memory only (not in sessionStorage), or use HttpOnly cookies if possible. 2. Implement rate limiting and account lockout on failed login attempts to mitigate brute-force attacks. 3. Set CSP header to restrict script sources (reduces XSS risk). 4. Consider adding a token expiration mechanism (e.g., tokens expire after 1 hour, requiring re-authentication). 5. Investigate using Web Crypto API for in-memory token management.
current_finding.impact: XSS vulnerability leading to token theft could allow attackers to impersonate users and access deployments, violating ISO 27001 A.8.2 (authentication) and GDPR Art. 32 (security). Breached tokens could expose sensitive deployment data.

current_finding.title: No rate limiting on authentication endpoints
current_finding.description: The /api/auth/verify endpoint accepts unlimited auth attempts without rate limiting, enabling brute-force attacks against the passphrase. An attacker could try thousands of passphrases per second until one succeeds.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Add rate limiting to /api/auth/verify: implement exponential backoff (e.g., 5 attempts per minute, then 1 attempt per 5 minutes after 5 failed attempts). 2. Use Flask-Limiter library: from flask_limiter import Limiter; limiter.limit('5/minute')(auth_verify). 3. Log repeated failures and alert on suspicious patterns. 4. Consider implementing account lockout: temporary lock after N failed attempts.
current_finding.impact: Brute-force attacks could compromise the web UI, allowing unauthorized access to deployment management, violating ISO 27001 A.8.3 and GDPR Art. 32 security requirements.

current_finding.title: Limited input validation on requestor name
current_finding.description: The requestor field is validated only for non-emptiness and presence of valid characters (line 189), but no length limits or content restrictions are enforced. A malicious requestor could submit extremely long strings or special characters that could cause issues in logging or downstream systems.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: 1. Add maximum length validation: len(requestor) <= 255. 2. Reject non-ASCII or control characters if unexpected. 3. Add validation for scenario field similarly.
current_finding.impact: Input validation bypasses could lead to log injection, DoS, or data corruption. While not directly a GDPR violation, it contributes to system instability that could cause data loss or unauthorized access.

current_finding.title: Missing data export mechanism for right to data portability
current_finding.description: GDPR Article 20 requires that data subjects have the right to receive their personal data in a structured, commonly used, machine-readable format and to transmit it to another controller. The system has account deletion but no data export endpoint that allows users to download their data (profile info, settings, saved content, notes, journals, bookmarks, plans, preferences, etc.) in a portable format like JSON or CSV.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Implement GET /api/export endpoint that: (1) authenticates the requesting user, (2) queries all user data from DynamoDB using the user ID, (3) assembles data into a JSON or CSV format with all personal data fields, (4) returns the export as a downloadable file. Include: user profile, all studies, all journals, all bookmarks, preferences, plan progress, streaks, and event-related data. Add a timestamp to the export showing when it was generated. Implement rate limiting to prevent abuse.
current_finding.impact: GDPR violations can result in fines up to €20 million or 4% of global annual revenue, whichever is higher. Non-compliance with data portability rights specifically triggers supervisory authority investigations and user complaints. Users in EU may file complaints with national Data Protection Authorities.

current_finding.title: Content Security Policy allows unsafe-inline and unsafe-eval for scripts
current_finding.description: The CSP header in CloudFront allows 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval' for script-src, which defeats the purpose of CSP and significantly increases the attack surface for Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) injection attacks. While unsafe-inline is sometimes necessary for SPA applications, the addition of unsafe-eval is particularly dangerous and should be avoided.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: (1) Remove 'unsafe-eval' immediately — there is almost never a legitimate reason for unsafe-eval. (2) For unsafe-inline: attempt to remove by using Content Security Policy nonces or hashes for inline scripts. If that's not feasible due to Next.js architecture, document why it's necessary and document compensating controls (e.g., strong input validation, output encoding). (3) Test that existing scripts still work with a stricter CSP (using test policy first). (4) Consider using a CSP violation reporting mechanism to catch injection attempts.
current_finding.impact: XSS vulnerabilities can lead to: account hijacking, data theft, malware injection. Not directly a GDPR violation but compromises confidentiality and integrity of personal data. ISO 27001 A.8.24 requires cryptographic and logical controls on data. CSP weakening is a control failure. Incident response would be required if XSS leads to data breach.
current_finding.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.24 (Cryptography & Key Management) and OWASP Top 10 (A03:2021 Injection)

current_finding.title: No granular consent mechanism for non-essential cookies and analytics
current_finding.description: The privacy policy mentions data collection and processing, but there is no cookie consent banner or granular consent mechanism visible on the site. The login page checks for GPC (Global Privacy Control) signal, but this is browser-level rather than site-specific consent. Third-party analytics (plausible.io is mentioned in CSP) may be loaded without explicit user consent. Without proper consent mechanisms, any non-essential cookies or analytics tracking violates GDPR requirements for lawful basis.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Implement a cookie consent solution: (1) Add a consent banner on first load (non-intrusive but visible) that allows users to: accept essential cookies only, or accept all including analytics/marketing. (2) Store consent choices in localStorage and HttpOnly cookie with a 12-month expiry. (3) Only load plausible.io analytics script if user consents. (4) Create a preference center (linked from footer) allowing users to change consent at any time. (5) Document in privacy policy what cookies are used and for what purpose. (6) Implement consent records in the database (which user consented to what, when, which privacy policy version).
current_finding.impact: GDPR Art. 6(1)(a) requires explicit consent for processing based on consent. Without granular consent, any analytics/marketing data processing is unlawful. Fines up to €10 million or 2% of revenue. Supervisory authorities actively enforce this via monitoring for missing consent banners.

current_finding.title: Operational log retention policy not implemented or documented
current_finding.description: The privacy policy states 'Operational logs are generally retained for about 30 days unless needed longer for security review' but there is no evidence of implemented data deletion or TTL mechanism in the codebase. DynamoDB table has a TTL attribute (expiresAt) but it appears to be used only for rate-limit entries and password reset codes, not for audit/operational logs. Without automated cleanup, logs could be retained indefinitely.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: (1) Add explicit TTL to all DynamoDB log entries: set expiresAt = current_time + 30 days when logging. (2) For S3 access logs, configure S3 Lifecycle Policy to delete objects after 30 days. (3) For CloudWatch Logs, set log group retention to 30 days. (4) For any application-level logging (e.g., to external service), implement rotation and deletion. (5) Implement a data retention policy document and review logs quarterly. (6) Ensure logging does not capture sensitive data (passwords, tokens, PII); if needed, hash or redact before logging.
current_finding.impact: GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) requires data be kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than necessary. Indefinite retention of logs (especially containing IP addresses, user IDs, request metadata) violates storage limitation. Regulatory exposure if audit shows logs retained beyond stated 30-day period.

current_finding.title: Logs may contain sensitive data without documented sanitization
current_finding.description: The logging function (logAuthEvent at line 295-301) logs to console without explicitly excluding sensitive fields. While passwords are hashed in the database, other sensitive information like email addresses, user IDs, and request metadata are logged. The password reset code is stored in plaintext in DynamoDB (line 755), and error messages in API responses may leak information about user existence (though email enumeration is partially protected).
current_finding.fix_suggestion: (1) Define a logging policy: document which fields are safe to log (timestamps, event names, anonymized/hashed identifiers). (2) Implement log sanitization: before logging any field, check against a 'safe fields' whitelist. (3) Hash or redact PII before logging. (4) Hash password reset codes in DynamoDB (use timing-safe comparison on verification like passwords). (5) Test that logs do not contain passwords, tokens, or plaintext PII. (6) Document retention and access controls for logs (who can view, from where).
current_finding.impact: If logs are breached, exposed PII violates GDPR Art. 32 (security of processing) and Art. 33 (breach notification). Exposed email addresses + failed login attempts enable targeted phishing. ISO 27001 A.8.15 requires logging of access and changes; A.8.16 requires audit trail protection.
current_finding.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.15 & A.8.16 (Logging & Audit Trails) and A.8.25 (Input Validation)

current_finding.title: Multi-factor authentication not implemented despite security claims
current_finding.description: The README and privacy policy describe the system as secure and trustworthy, but there is no MFA support. In a system handling event organizer data and attendee information (PII), MFA should be available for users with administrative access or handling sensitive event data. Current single-factor authentication (password + email verification) is insufficient for a B2B platform.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: (1) Implement optional TOTP (Time-based One-Time Password) using libraries like speakeasy or similar. (2) Add API endpoints: POST /api/auth/mfa/setup (returns QR code), POST /api/auth/mfa/verify (validates TOTP), POST /api/auth/mfa/disable (requires current password). (3) Require MFA for admin accounts (ADMIN_EMAILS set at line 16-18). (4) Add MFA state to token (mfaVerified flag). (5) After login, if MFA is enabled, require TOTP verification before returning full access token. (6) Provide backup recovery codes for account access if authenticator app is lost.
current_finding.impact: Weak authentication increases risk of unauthorized access to organizer event data, attendee contact information, and booth/vendor data. While not directly a GDPR violation, compromised accounts lead to breaches. ISO 27001 A.8.2 recommends MFA for sensitive systems. Regulatory bodies increasingly expect MFA for business-critical accounts.

current_finding.title: No Data Protection Impact Assessment documented for event data processing
current_finding.description: The system processes personal data of event organizers, attendees, speakers, vendors, and booth managers. This includes names, emails, event attendance information, photos (gallery feature), and potentially sensitive role/access information. GDPR Art. 35 requires a DPIA where processing is likely to result in high risk. While the README mentions the app stores 'event and organizer data such as saved local notes, preview settings, bookmarks, and preferences,' there's no documented DPIA or risk assessment.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: (1) Conduct a DPIA covering: event photo gallery (collection of attendee photos), role-based access (tracking who has admin/organizer permissions), event attendance tracking, email marketing (event reminders), third-party data sharing (if any). (2) Document: data flow, purposes, retention, recipients, rights of data subjects, technical/organizational safeguards, risks, and mitigations. (3) Store DPIA in a protected document (not public) but be prepared to share with supervisory authorities if requested. (4) If high-risk processing is found, document consultation with DPO if applicable. (5) Review DPIA annually or when processing significantly changes.
current_finding.impact: If processing is high-risk and no DPIA is conducted, GDPR Art. 35 is violated. Supervisory authorities specifically check for DPIAs when investigating complaints about high-risk processing (e.g., photo galleries, role-based access, event attendance tracking).
current_finding.regulation: GDPR Art. 25 (Data Protection by Design and Default) & Art. 35 (DPIA)

current_finding.title: Backup and disaster recovery procedures not documented
current_finding.description: While DynamoDB has point-in-time recovery enabled (terraform/dynamodb.tf line 43-45), there is no documented backup strategy, recovery time objectives (RTO), recovery point objectives (RPO), or disaster recovery runbook. No mention of backup testing or verification procedures. Incident response procedures are not documented in the codebase.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: (1) Document RTO and RPO: define acceptable downtime and data loss (e.g., RTO 4 hours, RPO 1 hour). (2) Enable DynamoDB Exports to S3 on a daily schedule (via Lambda + CloudWatch Events). (3) Implement S3 cross-region replication for critical backups. (4) Document backup retention: keep 7 days of daily exports, 4 weeks of weekly exports. (5) Test restore procedures quarterly and document results. (6) Create incident response runbook: breach (immediate steps, notification, investigation), data loss (restore from backup), service outage (failover plan). (7) Assign incident commander and escalation contacts.
current_finding.impact: ISO 27001 A.8.13 requires backup and recovery capability. GDPR breach notification (Art. 33) requires documented incident response with specific timelines (72 hours). Without documented procedures, response times will be slow and recovery may not meet user expectations (e.g., attendees unable to access event during outage).
current_finding.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.13 (Backup & Recovery) and A.5.28 (Incident Response)

current_finding.title: Cache-Control headers not explicitly set for sensitive endpoints
current_finding.description: CloudFront default cache behavior (terraform/cloudfront.tf line 159-182) does not explicitly set Cache-Control headers for sensitive pages like login, account settings, and data views. Browser caching of sensitive content could expose user data if the browser cache is accessed by another user on a shared computer.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: (1) Add Cache-Control header to CloudFront response headers policy for sensitive paths (/login, /settings, /e/*/manage, /account). (2) For these paths: Cache-Control: no-store, no-cache, must-revalidate, max-age=0. (3) For public/marketing pages (/features, /pricing, /contact): Cache-Control: public, max-age=3600. (4) Test that sensitive pages are not cached by inspecting response headers. (5) Document cache policy in security documentation.
current_finding.impact: User data cached in browser history/cache could be exposed if computer is shared. Not a direct GDPR/ISO violation but a data security risk. Affects confidentiality of personal data on shared devices (e.g., library, office shared computer).
current_finding.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.9 (Security Headers) and A.8.32 (Change Management)

current_finding.title: Admin functionality not restricted by role-based access control
current_finding.description: Admin access is determined by checking email membership in a hardcoded ADMIN_EMAILS set (lambda/api/index.mjs line 16-18) containing only 'cody@codyjo.com'. This is not scalable and lacks audit trail. There is no granular role-based access control (RBAC) system. As the system grows and additional staff need access, admin privileges cannot be delegated or revoked via UI.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: (1) Create a ROLES table: id, userId, role (admin, organizer, viewer, etc.), grantedAt, grantedBy, revokedAt. (2) Replace hardcoded ADMIN_EMAILS with database query: queryCommand for USER#X/ROLE. (3) Add admin UI: list users, grant/revoke roles, view audit log. (4) Log all role changes and admin actions: CREATE event with adminUserId, targetUserId, action, before/after state. (5) Implement role-based permission checks for all sensitive endpoints. (6) Document roles and their capabilities. (7) Require re-authentication for sensitive admin operations (password prompt).
current_finding.impact: Lack of granular access control violates ISO 27001 A.8.5 (access control) principle. If admin credentials are compromised, all data is at risk. No ability to audit who made what change (violates A.8.16 audit trails for changes).
current_finding.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.5 (Access Control) and A.5.9 (Principle of Least Privilege)

current_finding.title: JWT secret rotation policy not documented
current_finding.description: JWT secret is stored in AWS Secrets Manager (lambda/api/index.mjs line 25-36) and cached after cold start. There is no documented rotation policy. If the secret is compromised, old tokens remain valid until expiry (30 days per line 178). Lack of key rotation guidance increases risk of long-term compromise.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: (1) Document JWT secret rotation policy: rotate every 90 days (or immediately if compromise suspected). (2) Implement key versioning in Secrets Manager: store both current and previous secrets, try current first, fall back to previous if verification fails. (3) Create a rotation Lambda triggered by EventBridge on a 90-day schedule. (4) Shorter token expiry: consider 1-7 days instead of 30 days, requiring refresh tokens for longer sessions. (5) Implement token revocation list (denylist) for compromised tokens or explicit logout. (6) Document rotation procedure and test it in a dev environment.
current_finding.impact: If JWT secret is compromised, attacker can forge tokens valid for 30 days. ISO 27001 A.8.24 recommends key rotation. GDPR Art. 32 requires technical measures appropriate to the risk.
current_finding.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.24 (Key Management) and A.5.12 (Access Control)

current_finding.title: Privacy information not provided for event attendees registered by organizers
current_finding.description: The system allows event organizers to import or register attendees (guest list, booth manager endpoints visible). When an attendee is registered by an organizer (not self-registration), the attendee may not receive privacy information about how their data is processed. GDPR Art. 14 requires disclosure of controller identity, purposes, recipients, rights, etc. when data is collected indirectly.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: (1) When an organizer adds an attendee to an event (guest list, booth), send an email to the attendee with: controller identity (Cordivent), purposes (event coordination, attendance tracking), retention period, rights (access, deletion, portability). (2) Include a link to the full privacy policy and unsubscribe link. (3) Log that notification was sent. (4) Provide organizers with a template/guidance on what privacy info to include when manually inviting attendees. (5) In-app notification option for attendees already in the system: notify them that they were added to [event name] and link to privacy info.
current_finding.impact: GDPR Art. 14 violation if attendees are not informed when added to events. Supervisory authority would expect documentation that privacy info was provided, either via email or in-app notification.
current_finding.regulation: GDPR Art. 14 (Information to be provided where personal data are not obtained from the data subject)

current_finding.title: Unclear lawful basis for processing reading interpretations and analytics
current_finding.description: While registration requires consent to the privacy policy, the privacy policy does not clearly articulate the specific lawful basis for each processing activity. The policy mentions sending reading context to Anthropic (Claude API) as 'automated decision-making support you explicitly request', but does not establish a clear legal basis (consent, contract, legitimate interest, etc.) for collecting and processing analytics data (IP address, browser metadata, rate-limit events). Art. 6 requires a lawful basis for every processing activity.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Revise the privacy policy to explicitly state the lawful basis for each major processing activity: (1) Account data and readings: Contract (Art. 6(1)(b)) as necessary for service provision; (2) Analytics: Legitimate interest (Art. 6(1)(f)) - specify the legitimate interests being served; (3) Email communications: Consent for marketing, contract for transactional. Create a detailed processing activity table or data mapping document. Ensure GDPR Art. 13 transparency for all collection points.
current_finding.impact: GDPR violations can result in fines up to 4% of annual turnover or €20 million. Lack of clear lawful basis for all processing is a foundational compliance gap that could expose the organization to enforcement action by supervisory authorities.

current_finding.title: No age verification or parental consent mechanism for users under 16
current_finding.description: The privacy policy explicitly states 'intended only for people who are at least 16 years old' and 'not directed to children under 13', but there is no age gate, age verification, or parental consent mechanism at registration. A user can register with any age and the system does not verify or restrict access. Under GDPR Art. 8, processing of children's data under 16 requires parental consent (in most EU member states). Under COPPA (for US users), children under 13 require verifiable parental consent. Under California's AADC (AB 2273), businesses likely accessed by children must implement age-appropriate design and protections. The absence of enforcement allows minors to create accounts and have their data processed without proper safeguards.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Implement one of the following: (1) Age gate at registration requiring DOB entry and verification (reject users under 16); (2) Parental consent flow for users 13-16 (requires email verification from parent); (3) Age estimation using third-party service (Yoti, AgeChecker, etc.). For US users, implement COPPA compliance: age verification, parental consent for <13, verifiable parental email. Document compliance in privacy policy and DPIA. Consider geographic detection to apply jurisdiction-specific rules. Add prominent age disclaimer on login/registration pages.
current_finding.impact: GDPR violations for processing children's data without parental consent: up to 4% of turnover. COPPA violations: up to $43,280 per violation. CCPA/AADC violations: up to $2,500-$7,500 per violation. Enforcement action by FTC, state AGs, and EU supervisory authorities. Loss of user trust and reputational damage.
current_finding.regulation: GDPR Art. 8, COPPA (15 U.S.C. § 6501-6506), California AADC (AB 2273)

current_finding.title: No documented Data Processing Agreements with third-party processors
current_finding.description: The privacy policy discloses that reading context is sent to Anthropic (Claude API) for interpretation and that AWS and Resend are used for hosting and email. However, there is no evidence of documented Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) in place with these vendors. Under GDPR Art. 28, any processor who handles personal data on behalf of a controller must have a written DPA in place that specifies: processor obligations, instructions for data processing, confidentiality, security measures, data subject rights support, and subprocessor authorization. The absence of DPAs is a direct violation of GDPR.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Obtain or review DPAs from: (1) Anthropic for Claude API usage; (2) AWS for S3, Lambda, DynamoDB, CloudFront, Secrets Manager services; (3) Resend for email service. Ensure DPAs include: data subject request support, subprocessor notification, deletion/return of data on termination, audit rights, data security obligations. Create a Data Processing Register documenting all processors. Link DPA summaries in the privacy policy or provide on request.
current_finding.impact: GDPR Art. 28 violation: supervisory authority can issue corrective orders and fines up to 10 million euros or 2% of annual turnover (whichever is higher). Personal liability for data protection officers and controllers. Cannot demonstrate processor compliance to auditors or regulators.

current_finding.title: Incomplete disclosure of international data transfers and adequacy decisions
current_finding.description: The privacy policy states 'The app is hosted primarily on Amazon Web Services in the United States' but does not explicitly inform EU users that their data is transferred outside the EEA. GDPR Art. 13 requires controllers to inform data subjects of transfers outside the EEA and the legal mechanism ensuring adequate protection (e.g., Standard Contractual Clauses, adequacy decision, Binding Corporate Rules). While the US is not designated as having adequate protection under GDPR, transfers can proceed under SCCs or other mechanisms. The privacy policy must inform users of this transfer and the safeguards in place.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Revise privacy policy to include: (1) Explicit statement that personal data is transferred to and processed in the United States by AWS, Anthropic, and Resend; (2) Statement that the US is not deemed to have adequate data protection by the European Commission; (3) Explanation of safeguards: Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) in place with US processors, compliance with Schrems II supplementary measures where applicable; (4) Link to Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) or summary; (5) EU users' rights to request supplementary safeguards; (6) Contact information for data transfer inquiries. Ensure SCCs are in place with all US processors.
current_finding.impact: GDPR violation if adequate safeguards are not in place or not disclosed. Fines up to 10 million euros or 2% of turnover. Supervisory authority enforcement. Potential data subject claims for damages (Art. 82). User distrust.

current_finding.title: Missing explicit API rate limiting on resource-intensive endpoints
current_finding.description: While rate limiting is implemented for authentication endpoints (registration: 3/hour, login: 5/15min, account deletion: 5/15min), there is no evidence of rate limiting on resource-intensive API endpoints like POST /api/interpret (AI interpretation) and POST /api/readings/share (public share generation). These endpoints could be abused to: (1) Generate high numbers of API calls to Anthropic (cost and DoS); (2) Create numerous share tokens; (3) Exceed DynamoDB capacity. Without rate limiting, a malicious user can cause service degradation or unexpected costs.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Implement rate limiting on POST endpoints: (1) POST /api/interpret: limit to 10 interpretations per user per hour; (2) POST /api/readings: limit to 20 readings per user per day; (3) POST /api/readings/share: limit to 5 shares per user per hour; (4) POST /api/journals: limit to 10 journals per user per day. Use the existing checkRateLimit() function pattern. Log rate limit events for monitoring. Consider using x-ratelimit headers in responses to inform clients of limits.
current_finding.impact: ISO 27001 violation (lack of access control and use limitation). Potential for DDoS or cost-based attacks. Operational risk: service unavailability, unexpected cloud costs, harm to users' service experience.

current_finding.title: Incomplete audit logging and missing security event coverage
current_finding.description: While the application logs some security events (auth_register_duplicate, data_export, auth_account_delete, auth_password_reset), audit logging is not comprehensive. Missing audit coverage includes: (1) Successful logins and login failures (only failed registrations logged); (2) Email changes and password changes (code has logging for delete, but grep confirms no logging for changePassword/changeEmail endpoints); (3) Journal access and reads; (4) Admin API access and actions; (5) Data modification events (reading creation, updates); (6) Encryption key generation/recovery. Audit trails must be immutable, timestamped, and retention policies defined.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Expand audit logging to cover all security-relevant events: (1) Authentication: successful login, failed login, password change, email change, account deletion; (2) Authorization: failed access attempts to protected endpoints, admin actions; (3) Data access: journal reads (consider if necessary), export data requests; (4) Encryption: key generation, key recovery attempts, key rotation; (5) Third-party integrations: AI interpretation requests sent to Anthropic; (6) Sensitive operations: account modifications, reading sharing. Log: event type, user ID, timestamp, IP address, result (success/failure). Do not log passwords, tokens, or sensitive data. Implement log retention policy (e.g., 90 days for operational logs, 1 year for security logs). Consider shipping logs to CloudWatch Logs with long-term S3 archival for immutability.
current_finding.impact: ISO 27001 A.8.15/A.8.16 non-compliance. Inability to detect security incidents or trace unauthorized access. Breach detection and investigation hindered. Regulatory bodies expect comprehensive audit logs for compliance audits.
current_finding.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.15, A.8.16 (Logging and Audit Trails)

current_finding.title: Vague data retention policy for operational logs
current_finding.description: The privacy policy states 'Operational logs are generally retained for about 30 days unless needed longer for security review' (README.md: line 89). This is vague and does not meet GDPR transparency requirements. GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) requires that personal data be kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than necessary. The policy should specify: (1) exact retention periods for different data categories; (2) how retention is enforced (TTL, scheduled cleanup, manual deletion); (3) when 'longer retention for security review' is triggered; (4) what happens after retention period expires.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Establish and document explicit retention schedules: (1) Operational logs (auth, API errors): 30 days auto-expiry via CloudWatch Logs retention; (2) Security-relevant logs (failures, deletions, exports): 90 days; (3) Billing/audit logs: 7 years (for regulatory/tax purposes); (4) User data (readings, journals): indefinite until user deletion. Implement retention via CloudWatch Logs retention policies, DynamoDB TTL, and S3 lifecycle policies. Document in privacy policy and operational runbooks. Update Terraform to enforce retention.
current_finding.impact: GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) violation: storage limitation principle not met. Fines up to 10 million euros or 2% of annual turnover. Data subjects cannot trust retention policies.

current_finding.title: Potential exposure of sensitive data in logs
current_finding.description: While the application is careful not to log full tokens or passwords, the current logging approach logs user IDs and IP addresses for audit purposes. If logs are exposed or accessed by unauthorized parties, these can be correlated to identify users. Additionally, CloudWatch Logs and application error responses may expose sensitive information: (1) Error messages may leak internal API structure or backend details; (2) Exception stack traces in development might expose code paths; (3) CloudWatch Logs are accessible to IAM-permitted users without end-to-end encryption.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Implement comprehensive log handling: (1) Mask or hash sensitive data in logs: user IDs can be hashed, IP addresses can be anonymized (truncate last octet); (2) Use structured logging (JSON) for easier parsing and filtering; (3) Implement log access controls: restrict CloudWatch Logs access to authorized personnel via IAM; (4) Enable CloudWatch Logs encryption at rest (already available in AWS); (5) Set log retention for CloudWatch Logs to 30-90 days; (6) Never log request/response bodies containing sensitive data; (7) Use AWS VPC endpoints to route logs securely; (8) Document log access and audit logs.
current_finding.impact: ISO 27001 non-compliance. Potential data breach if logs are exposed. GDPR risk if user IDs and IPs are logged without strong access controls and retention limits. Reputational damage.

current_finding.title: No age-appropriate design considerations for minor users
current_finding.description: While the app is 'not directed to children', if minors access the app, there are no design safeguards to protect their interests. GDPR Art. 8 and California AADC (AB 2273) require age-appropriate design for services likely to be used by children. This includes: (1) Default high-privacy settings for minors; (2) No dark patterns (infinite scroll, autoplay, addictive design); (3) Prominent, accessible privacy information in language suitable for children; (4) No profiling or behavioral targeting of minors; (5) Restrictions on data sharing with third parties. The app has no age-specific safeguards.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: If the app is intentionally not targeting minors, consider: (1) Adding prominent age gate and terms confirming 16+ access only; (2) If minor access is anticipated, implement age-appropriate mode: high-privacy defaults, disable data sharing, no behavioral tracking, simplified UI with large text, accessible privacy information; (3) Parental dashboard for minors 13-16 (parental approval required); (4) Restrict features available to minors (e.g., no public sharing, limits on data export); (5) No marketing or promotional content to minors. Document compliance in privacy policy and DPIA.
current_finding.impact: GDPR Art. 8 and AADC (AB 2273) non-compliance if minors use the service. Fines up to €20 million or 4% of turnover (GDPR). FTC enforcement and state AG fines for COPPA/AADC violations ($2,500-$7,500 per violation).
current_finding.regulation: GDPR Art. 8, COPPA (Children's Online Privacy Protection Act)

current_finding.title: Incomplete documentation of backup and disaster recovery procedures
current_finding.description: The DynamoDB configuration includes point-in-time recovery (PITR) enabled, which is excellent. However, there is no documented backup strategy, recovery time objective (RTO), recovery point objective (RPO), or tested recovery procedures. ISO 27001 A.8.13 requires documented backup and recovery procedures with regular testing.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Document and implement: (1) Backup strategy: DynamoDB PITR (current), daily full backups to S3, cross-region replication for failover; (2) Define RTO and RPO: RTO <4 hours, RPO <1 hour; (3) Recovery procedures: documented runbooks for DynamoDB recovery, S3 data recovery, Lambda redeployment; (4) Test recovery quarterly: execute full restore from backup to test environment; (5) Communicate backup and recovery procedures to ops team; (6) Monitor backup success via CloudWatch alarms; (7) Document in runbooks and architecture documentation.
current_finding.impact: ISO 27001 non-compliance. Risk of data loss in case of major incident. Service unavailability. Reputational damage.

current_finding.title: CSP allows unsafe-inline scripts (acceptable but verbose)
current_finding.description: The Content Security Policy (CSP) in Terraform (cloudfront.tf, line 235) includes 'script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' https://plausible.io'. While 'unsafe-inline' is necessary for Plausible analytics initialization and is from a trusted third party, it reduces the effectiveness of CSP against XSS attacks. Additionally, the CSP allows inline styles ('style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline''), which could be exploited. This is a minor issue but represents a defense-in-depth opportunity.
current_finding.fix_suggestion: Consider: (1) Moving Plausible initialization to an external script file to remove need for 'unsafe-inline'; (2) Using Content Security Policy nonces for any unavoidable inline scripts; (3) Restricting style-src to 'self' only and using CSS classes instead of inline styles; (4) Adding script-src hash if Plausible init code is fixed. For now, 'unsafe-inline' is acceptable given the trusted nature of Plausible, but evaluate in future security reviews.
current_finding.impact: Minor ISO 27001 non-compliance. Slight increase in XSS risk if inline script injection vulnerabilities are present in dependencies.

current_finding.title: Freemium SaaS subscription model with tiered storage and gallery limits
current_finding.description: Implement three pricing tiers: Free (1 active gallery, 100 photos max, basic stats), Pro ($39/month, 10 galleries, 5K photos, advanced analytics, custom domain), Studio ($79/month, unlimited galleries, unlimited photos, API access, white-label). Free tier drives adoption and network effects; paid tiers monetize active photographers with real delivery volume.

current_finding.title: Freemium SaaS Model with Tiered Subscriptions
current_finding.description: Implement a freemium SaaS model with three subscription tiers: Free (1 repo, manual scans only), Starter ($49/month, up to 10 repos, scheduled scans, basic support), Pro ($199/month, unlimited repos, custom agents, API access, white-label dashboards), and Enterprise (custom pricing for on-premises deployment). The existing API server and dashboard infrastructure are production-ready for this implementation. Target audience: development teams (SMB to mid-market) and enterprises managing multiple codebases.

current_finding.title: API Tier and Per-Request Billing
current_finding.description: Monetize API access through usage-based billing: $0.01-0.05 per scan, $5-25/month minimum for API tier subscribers. Include request quotas (100 scans/month for free, unlimited for Pro). This targets development teams integrating Back Office into their CI/CD workflows (pre-commit hooks, PR gating, scheduled audits). Existing API server (`backoffice/api_server.py`) already supports this; add authentication and metering.

current_finding.title: Custom Agent Development & Audit Template Service
current_finding.description: Offer professional services to build custom audit agents for specialized tech stacks or compliance requirements. Example: 'Kubernetes Audit Agent', 'Healthcare HIPAA Audit Agent', 'Financial Services Audit Agent'. Price at $2,000-10,000 per custom agent, with ongoing support at $500-2,000/month. Also offer pre-built audit template packs ($99-499) targeting specific frameworks (Next.js, FastAPI, React Native, etc.). This leverages the existing agent infrastructure and expertise.

current_finding.title: White-Label Reseller Program & Platform Licensing
current_finding.description: License Back Office to agencies, consultancies, and tool vendors as a white-label product. They resell audits under their own branding with their own pricing. Back Office takes 30-40% commission on scans or charges a $2,000-5,000/month platform license. Target: code review shops, DevOps consultancies, dev agencies. This expands reach without direct marketing. The dashboard infrastructure already supports custom branding (site-branding.js exists).

current_finding.title: Premium Implementation & Consulting Service
current_finding.description: Offer implementation consulting at $150-250/hour: onboarding teams, interpreting findings, integrating into CI/CD pipelines, setting compliance baselines, designing remediation workflows. Also offer fixed-price 'Audit Baseline' projects ($5,000-15,000) where we scan a large codebase, provide deep analysis, and hand off a findings matrix with remediation roadmap. Target: enterprises, regulated industries, companies with complex codebases.

current_finding.title: Premium Educational Content & Certifications
current_finding.description: Create paid video courses on 'Code Audit Best Practices', 'Building Custom Audit Agents', 'Compliance Auditing for Healthcare/Finance'. Offer $49-199 per course. Also create certifications (e.g., 'Cody Jo Back Office Certified Auditor') at $299 with exam and badge. Target: individual developers, team leads, consultants. Host on Teachable, Udemy, or standalone platform.

current_finding.title: Advanced Analytics & Insights Dashboard
current_finding.description: Premium feature: historical trend analysis, regression detection, finding predictions, team productivity metrics, and custom reports. Show: 'code quality trending up 15% over 3 months', 'new bugs introduced per developer', 'time to fix by severity'. This adds strategic value for CTOs and drives higher-tier upgrades. Requires storing historical scan data (currently not persisted).

current_finding.title: Scheduled & Automated Scanning with Smart Defaults
current_finding.description: Free tier: manual scans only. Starter/Pro tiers: scheduled scans (daily, weekly) with auto-remediation suggestions. Include smart defaults: 'aggressive mode' (max findings, all departments) vs 'lightweight mode' (critical only, faster). This encourages adoption and distinguishes tiers. Existing infrastructure (agent scripts, cron) supports this.

current_finding.title: Managed Scan Service for Enterprises
current_finding.description: Enterprise add-on: Back Office team manages scans, interprets findings, and produces weekly summary reports (executive summary + detailed findings). Priced at $3,000-10,000/month depending on number of repos and SLA. Target: enterprises without internal DevOps capacity. This is a high-touch, high-margin service.

current_finding.title: Audit Template Marketplace (Template-as-a-Service)
current_finding.description: Create a marketplace for pre-built audit templates ($99-499 each): 'Next.js Best Practices', 'Python Security Audit', 'GraphQL API Audit', 'Kubernetes Config Audit', 'GDPR Compliance Audit'. Developers/agencies buy templates, customize for their stack, and reuse. Revenue model: 70/30 split with template creators, or direct sales if created in-house. This builds a library of reusable IP.

current_finding.title: Single Sign-On (SSO) & Okta/Azure AD Integration
current_finding.description: Enterprise feature: SSO via Okta, Azure AD, Google Workspace. Allows enterprises to provision users in bulk and manage permissions centrally. Charged as add-on ($500-2,000/month) or included in Enterprise tier. This is table-stakes for enterprise sales.

current_finding.title: Scan Report Export & Custom Report Builder
current_finding.description: Premium feature: export findings in multiple formats (JSON, CSV, PDF, XML). Include custom report builder UI to select departments, severity levels, and formatting. Target compliance officers and auditors who need to share findings with stakeholders/regulators. Charged as add-on or included in Pro tier.

current_finding.title: DevTools Affiliate Partnerships
current_finding.description: Recommend complementary tools in Back Office dashboards: GitHub Advanced Security, Snyk, SonarQube, Dependabot, Vercel, Netlify, etc. Earn 10-20% commission on referred customers. Place affiliate links in: email reports, dashboard recommendations, knowledge base articles. This is passive revenue that doesn't affect user experience if done carefully.

current_finding.title: Framework Vendor & Tool Partnership Sponsorships
current_finding.description: Partner with framework vendors (React, Next.js, FastAPI, Django) to create co-branded audit templates and promote them in their docs/communities. In exchange, they promote Back Office to their user base. Examples: 'React Best Practices Audit powered by Back Office', 'Next.js Security Checklist'. This is brand-building but also drives free organic traffic and user acquisition.

current_finding.title: Back Office Logo & Merchandise Store
current_finding.description: Low-value but fun: create a merchandise store (Bonfire, Printful) selling Back Office-branded gear: t-shirts, hoodies, stickers, mugs. Price at 2-3x cost. This is brand awareness and community building more than revenue. Low effort to set up via Printful (drop-shipping).

current_finding.title: Open Source Sponsorship & GitHub Sponsors Program
current_finding.description: Sponsor open source projects and developer communities that Back Office benefits from (e.g., Python, Node.js, security projects) and/or run a GitHub Sponsors page. This is brand building and good optics, not direct revenue. Cost: $500-2,000/month to sponsor projects. Back Office position itself as 'giving back to the community'.

current_finding.title: Team Collaboration Features (Comments, Assignments, Workflows)
current_finding.description: Add collaboration to Back Office: comment on findings, assign to team members, create remediation workflows, track status. Priced as Pro/Enterprise feature. This makes Back Office a more complete DevOps platform and increases stickiness. Medium effort, moderate ROI.

current_finding.title: Browser Extension for GitHub/GitLab Integration
current_finding.description: Create a browser extension that injects Back Office scan results into GitHub/GitLab pull requests and commits. Show findings inline with code. This is a UI/UX enhancement and brand visibility tool. Free to users, generates affiliate revenue if it drives SaaS signups. Requires minimal development (wrapper around existing API).

current_finding.title: Launch a Selah Plus subscription around AI study, sync, and premium habit features
current_finding.description: Selah already has the core ingredients for a freemium SaaS model: authenticated accounts, rate-limited AI study, account backup, encryption, and analytics-friendly page structure. The strongest monetization path is a paid Selah Plus tier that keeps core Bible reading free while gating higher-cost and higher-perceived-value features such as higher AI study limits, synced reading plans across devices, offline study packs, premium wallpaper themes, and deeper insights. A realistic starting point is free with basic reading, bookmarks, journals, and limited AI; Plus at $7.99/month or $69/year with 5x-10x AI volume, sync, and premium habit tooling.

current_finding.title: Sell AI study packs or usage overages instead of forcing every heavy user into a higher plan
current_finding.description: The AI Lambda already rate limits requests per user and per IP, which creates a clean bridge to metered monetization. Offer a free monthly AI allowance, include a larger allowance in Selah Plus, and let users buy small top-up packs such as 50 or 100 extra study prompts. This reduces churn from occasional power users who do not want a full subscription and helps align margin with Anthropic usage cost.

current_finding.title: Create church and small-group plans with leader dashboards
current_finding.description: Selah's current individual study, journaling, plans, and AI features can be extended into a church or group subscription. The monetizable unit is the leader, church staff member, or Bible study organizer rather than the individual believer. A group plan could include shareable reading plans, discussion prompts, attendance or completion insights, and a branded invite flow for 10-100 members. A practical launch price is $19-49/month for small groups and $99+/month for churches using leader features.

current_finding.title: Offer a voluntary supporter membership to fund free access
current_finding.description: A faith product can monetize without hard paywalls by offering a supporter tier framed around sustaining free Bible reading for everyone. This is especially useful while traffic and retention are still maturing. Benefits can stay modest: supporter badge, monthly founder note, early access to new study styles, and a few premium themes. Suggested pricing is $3-5/month or $30-40/year.

current_finding.title: Build a contextual Christian resource shelf with affiliate links
current_finding.description: Affiliate revenue fits Selah better than display ads because it can stay respectful and context-driven. Add a small 'Recommended resources' shelf on public pages and in settings/help surfaces, pointing to study Bibles, devotional journals, commentaries, and prayer books relevant to the user's chosen reading plan or study style. Start with Christianbook and Amazon Associates because they cover the broadest Christian catalog. Keep links off deep reading surfaces unless they are clearly opt-in and separated from Scripture content.

current_finding.title: Use reading-plan and study-style pages to recommend higher-ticket study tools
current_finding.description: The best affiliate conversions will come from intent-matched recommendations, not generic banners. A user choosing academic study style can be shown an ESV Study Bible, commentary sets, or lexicon-friendly tools; a contemplative user can be shown prayer journals or devotional books; a church leader page can recommend group study books. Christianbook's catalog size and church-friendly positioning make it a strong first partner. This can later expand to Bible publishers or digital resource vendors.

current_finding.title: Sell seasonal devotional packs, printable guides, and guided reflection bundles
current_finding.description: Selah already has verse history, reading plans, reflections, and wallpaper generation. That can be packaged into digital seasonal products with low fulfillment overhead: Lent and Advent packs, 30-day anxiety or gratitude guides, church small-group discussion kits, printable verse cards, and premium wallpaper bundles. These are one-time purchases that can coexist with subscriptions and are easier to ship before full billing maturity.

current_finding.title: Do not run ads inside core reading and prayer flows; reserve advertising for a future public content hub only
current_finding.description: Display advertising is a weak fit for Selah's current product. The app has limited public content depth, sacred reading surfaces where ads would feel intrusive, and no dedicated blog or SEO article archive to support ad inventory. If a future public content hub is built, light ads could be tested on informational articles only, not on Daily, Books, Study, Journal, or Plans. That means ads are a long-tail add-on, not a primary business model.

current_finding.title: Offer sponsored seasonal reading plans and publisher-backed study experiences
current_finding.description: Sponsored plans are a better brand fit than banner ads. A publisher, conference, church network, or Christian nonprofit could sponsor a plan such as 'Advent with Isaiah' or '30 Days of Peace in the Psalms'. Sponsorship should be clearly labeled, editorially screened, and kept on opt-in plan pages rather than injected into active reading. Revenue can start as flat-fee sponsorships before ad-tech complexity enters the stack.

current_finding.title: Pursue white-label or co-branded Selah experiences for churches and ministries
current_finding.description: Longer term, Selah can move beyond direct-to-consumer revenue into branded ministry tooling. A church, conference, or Christian organization could license a co-branded sub-experience with custom plans, welcome screens, leader notes, and event follow-up flows. This is more valuable than basic sponsorship because it becomes recurring software revenue. The current app is not multi-tenant yet, so this is a platform play, not a quick win.

current_finding.title: AWS and Infrastructure Affiliate Programs
current_finding.description: The site and blog frequently reference cloud infrastructure, serverless functions (Lambda), S3, and CloudFront. Cody Jo's enterprise automation background makes credible recommendations for AWS services, VPS providers, and deployment tools. Target audience of technical builders actively makes these purchasing decisions.

current_finding.title: Managed PE Demo Provisioning Service (SaaS)
current_finding.description: Offer a white-labeled SaaS service where Puppet customers, resellers, and partners can provision PE demo environments on-demand without managing GCP infrastructure themselves. Cody Jo or the team acts as the infrastructure provider, handling billing, quota management, compliance, and multi-tenancy. Users pay per deployment or monthly subscription for unlimited deployments. Eliminates friction of GCP setup, IAM, billing coordination.

current_finding.title: Architecture Consulting & Custom Deployment Services
current_finding.description: Offer professional services for Puppet customers who need custom PE architectures not covered by the standard templates (e.g., multi-region HA, integration with existing infrastructure, compliance-specific setups like FIPS, airgapped environments). Charge hourly or per-engagement for architecture design, customization, deployment, and documentation. Position as 'PE Deployment Accelerators' for TAMs and sales team to upsell to customers.

current_finding.title: Freemium Model: Free Tier + Paid Tiers for Size/Features
current_finding.description: Introduce a freemium subscription model: Free tier for individuals/small labs (1 deployment, standard architecture, 7-day auto-destroy), Professional tier ($29/month: 5 concurrent deployments, all architectures, no auto-destroy), Enterprise tier ($99/month: unlimited deployments, priority support, advanced quota, custom retention). Monetize via subscription. Free tier drives adoption and community goodwill; paid tiers target teams and organizations.

current_finding.title: Premium Bolt Playbooks & Puppet Modules Marketplace
current_finding.description: Create a marketplace for premium, pre-built Bolt playbooks and Puppet modules that extend PE Bootstrap. Examples: 'PE Incident Response Playbook' ($49), 'Multi-Cloud Failover Module' ($99), 'Compliance Baseline (NIST/CIS)' ($79), 'Prometheus Monitoring Stack' ($69). Sell via Gumroad or a custom marketplace. These target operators who want to extend their PE labs beyond the base provisioning.

current_finding.title: Priority Support Tier (Email & Slack SLA)
current_finding.description: Offer a priority support tier ($49/month or $400/year) that includes guaranteed response SLAs (4-hour response for P1, 24-hour for P2). Includes Slack channel, direct email support, and quarterly architecture reviews. Differentiate from open-source community support. Target power users and organizations who can't wait for GitHub issue responses.

current_finding.title: PE Bootstrap Training & Certification Program
current_finding.description: Create an online training course (video + hands-on labs via PE Bootstrap) that teaches PE deployment patterns, PEADM architecture, and ops best practices. Offer it as a standalone course ($99-149) or bundle with consulting. Include a 'PE Bootstrap Practitioner' certification ($49 exam fee). Sell via Udemy, Coursera, or own Teachable site. Target ops teams and students.

current_finding.title: GCP Credits Affiliate Program & Cloud Cost Optimization
current_finding.description: Partner with Google Cloud to promote GCP in PE Bootstrap documentation and onboarding. Earn referral fees for customers who sign up for GCP via your affiliate link (typically $50-150 per signup + ongoing revenue share). Additionally, create a 'cost optimization guide' ($29 ebook or included in consulting) that teaches customers how to minimize GCP spend while running PE demos (spot instances, committed discounts, resource cleanup automation).

current_finding.title: Enterprise Managed Deployment Tier (Hands-On)
current_finding.description: Offer a premium tier where Cody Jo or a team member handles the full deployment on behalf of the customer. Customer provides GCP project + parameters; team handles VMs, networking, PE installation, post-deployment validation, and handoff. Priced as engagement fee ($2,000-5,000 per deployment) or retainer ($1,500/month for ongoing deployments + support). Targets enterprises who want white-glove service without internal expertise.

current_finding.title: Vertical-Specific PE Architecture Templates
current_finding.description: Sell pre-built architecture templates for specific industries/use cases: 'PE for K8s Operators' ($39), 'PE for Hybrid Cloud DevOps' ($49), 'PE for Compliance-Heavy Orgs' ($59). These are JSON/Terraform configs with industry-specific best practices, compliance mappings, and documentation. Sell via own store or marketplace. Low content creation (reuse existing docs).

current_finding.title: Cloud Vendor Sponsorships (GCP, HashiCorp, Red Hat)
current_finding.description: Approach GCP, HashiCorp, and Red Hat to sponsor PE Bootstrap documentation, training content, or community events. Sponsorship might include: logo placement on docs, featured blog posts, co-created case studies, or direct financial sponsorship ($1,000-2,000/quarter per sponsor). Sell the value: 'reach infrastructure automation professionals.'

current_finding.title: Disaster Recovery & Failover Runbooks (Industry-Specific)
